# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following derivations are **exam-oriented** and directly map to Part A (3-mark) and Part B (14-mark) KTU questions.

## 3.1 Derivation — Pozzolanic Reaction with Quantitative Mass Balance

Consider **1 kg of OPC** undergoing hydration. Approximate yield of $\text{Ca(OH)}_2$ (portlandite):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{C}_3\text{S} \text{ content of OPC} & \approx 50\% = 0.50 \text{ kg} \\
\text{Molar mass of C}_3\text{S} & \approx 228 \text{ g/mol} \\
\text{Moles of C}_3\text{S} & = 500 / 228 \approx 2.193 \text{ mol}
\end{aligned}
$$

The hydration reaction:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{C}_3\text{S} + (3+x)\text{H} \rightarrow \text{C-S-H}_x + 1.7 \text{ Ca(OH)}_2
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore, **moles of $\text{Ca(OH)}_2$ produced**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
n_{\text{CH}} = 2.193 \times 1.7 \approx 3.728 \text{ mol}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Mass of $\text{Ca(OH)}_2$** (molar mass $\approx 74 \text{ g/mol}$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{\text{CH}} = 3.728 \times 74 \approx 275.9 \text{ g} \approx 0.276 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

> So, **1 kg of OPC produces ~0.276 kg of portlandite** ($\text{Ca(OH)}_2$).

Now, if we **replace 20% of OPC with 0.20 kg of silica fume** ($\text{SiO}_2$, molar mass = 60 g/mol):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Moles of SiO}_2 & = 200 / 60 = 3.333 \text{ mol} \\
\text{Pozzolanic consumption of CH (1:1 molar basis):} & = 3.333 \text{ mol} \\
\text{Equivalent mass of CH consumed} & = 3.333 \times 74 = 246.7 \text{ g} \approx 0.247 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Resulting additional C-S-H** (assumed simplified C-S-H stoichiometry: $1.7 \text{ CH} + 1 \text{ S} \rightarrow 1 \text{ C-S-H}$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{\text{C-S-H formed}} & = (3.333 \text{ mol}) \times (\text{approx. } 150 \text{ g/mol}) \\
& = 500 \text{ g} = 0.50 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Engineering Insight:** Only 0.20 kg of silica fume creates ~0.50 kg of **secondary C-S-H gel** and consumes **0.247 kg of weak, soluble portlandite** — this is the chemical reason for ultra-high strength and low permeability in silica-fume concrete.

---

## 3.2 Step-by-Step — Water Reduction Calculation (Superplasticizer)

**Problem:** A concrete mix has w/c = 0.50 and uses 400 kg/m³ of cement. A PCE superplasticizer is added to reduce water by 30% while keeping slump constant at 100 mm. Find the new w/c and the **% increase in 28-day compressive strength** (using Abrams' Law approximation).

### Step 1 — Original water content

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_1 & = 0.50 \times 400 = 200 \text{ kg/m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Reduced water content (30% reduction)

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_2 & = 200 \times (1 - 0.30) = 140 \text{ kg/m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — New w/c ratio

$$
\begin{aligned}
(w/c)_2 & = 140 / 400 = 0.35
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Apply Abrams' Law (Simplified Empirical Form)

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_c = \frac{A}{B^{w/c}}
\end{aligned}
$$

Where for OPC concrete, $A \approx 985$ and $B \approx 7.0$ (typical empirical constants).

**Original strength:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{c1} = 985 / 7^{0.50} = 985 / 2.6458 \approx 372.3 \text{ kg/cm}^2 \approx 36.5 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**New strength:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{c2} = 985 / 7^{0.35} = 985 / 2.0902 \approx 471.2 \text{ kg/cm}^2 \approx 46.2 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**% Increase:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta f_c(\%) = \frac{46.2 - 36.5}{36.5} \times 100 \approx 26.6\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> **KTU Practical Takeaway:** A **30% water reduction** → w/c drops from 0.50 to 0.35 → strength jumps by **~27%** — this is the *quantitative* justification for superplasticizers in HPC.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Admixtures

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-yield comparative table — directly useful for KTU Part A "differentiate between" questions.**

| Parameter | Fly Ash (Class F) | Silica Fume | GGBS | RHA |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Main oxide | $\text{SiO}_2+\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3$ | $\text{SiO}_2$ | $\text{CaO}+\text{SiO}_2$ | $\text{SiO}_2$ |
| Reactivity | Low–Moderate | **Very High** | Moderate (latent) | High (if amorphous) |
| Particle size | 1–100 $\mu\text{m}$ | **0.1–0.5 $\mu\text{m}$** | 3–45 $\mu\text{m}$ | 5–30 $\mu\text{m}$ |
| Dosage (% cement) | 15–35 | 5–10 | 30–70 | 5–30 |
| Early strength | Lower | Higher | Slightly lower | Slightly lower |
| Ultimate strength | Higher | Much higher | Higher | Higher |
| Heat of hydration | Lowers **20–30%** | Neutral | Lowers **30–50%** | Lowers |
| Permeability | Reduces | **Drastically reduces** | Reduces | Reduces |
| ASR resistance | **Excellent** | Excellent | Good | Good |

| Parameter | Accelerator | Retarder | Plasticizer | Superplasticizer |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Main chemistry | $\text{CaCl}_2$ / nitrates | Sugars / lignosulphonates | Lignosulphonates | PCE / SNF / SMF |
| Dosage (% cement) | 1–2 | 0.1–0.5 | 0.1–0.4 | 0.2–2.0 |
| Water reduction | None | None | 5–12% | 16–40% |
| Setting time | Reduces | Increases | Marginal | Marginal |
| Slump effect | — | — | +25 to +75 mm | +100 to +200+ mm |
| Mechanism | Accelerates $\text{C}_3\text{A/S}$ hydration | Adsorbs on $\text{C}_3\text{S}$ surface | Electrostatic dispersion | **Steric + electrostatic** |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Q1. Define the term "admixture" as per IS 9103. Mention any two situations where retarders are essential in concrete construction.
> **[KTU University Exam — May 2023 Model]**

**Model Answer:**
- **Definition:** As per **IS 9103**, an admixture is a chemical or mineral material, other than cement, aggregate, water, and fibre reinforcement, added to the concrete mix **immediately before or during mixing** to modify one or more properties of the fresh or hardened concrete.
- **Two situations where retarders are essential:**
  1. **Hot-weather concreting** — to delay slump loss and early setting caused by high ambient temperatures.
  2. **Long-haul ready-mix concrete transport** — when transit time exceeds 60–90 minutes, retarders prevent premature stiffening.
  3. *(Optional additional point)* Large continuous mass pours where successive concrete lifts must remain plastic to bond properly.

---

### Q2. Differentiate between Class F and Class C fly ash based on calcium content and origin.
> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 Model]**

**Model Answer:**

| Property | Class F Fly Ash | Class C Fly Ash |
|---|---|---|
| **Origin** | Bituminous / Anthracite coal | Lignite / Sub-bituminous coal |
| **CaO content** | **< 10%** (low calcium) | **> 10%** (high calcium) |
| **Total $\text{SiO}_2 + \text{Al}_2\text{O}_3 + \text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3$** | $\geq 70\%$ | $\geq 50\%$ |
| **Reactivity** | Purely pozzolanic (needs $\text{Ca(OH)}_2$) | **Self-cementing** (hydraulic + pozzolanic) |
| **Setting** | Slows setting slightly | May accelerate early hydration |
| **Use** | Mass concrete, HPC, ASR mitigation | Cold-weather, early strength needs |

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Pattern)

> **Each sub-part = 7 marks | Total = 14 marks | Internal choice available**

### 🔹 Question A (Choice 1)

#### Q.A. (a) Explain the necessity of admixtures in modern concrete construction. Discuss in detail the pozzolanic reaction mechanism of silica fume with the help of a chemical equation.
> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]** *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**1. Necessity of Admixtures (4 key points, ≈ 3.5 marks):**

- **Workability enhancement without extra water:** Plasticizers/superplasticizers give flowing concrete at low w/c, critical for heavily reinforced sections and SCC.
- **Strength improvement:** Mineral admixtures (silica fume, fly ash, GGBS) produce additional C-S-H gel, raising long-term strength.
- **Durability:** Pozzolans densify the ITZ, reducing chloride and sulphate ingress, thereby extending service life of marine and bridge structures.
- **Economy & sustainability:** Replacing costly clinker with fly ash / GGBS reduces cost by 10–20% and embodied $\text{CO}_2$ by 30–50%.
- **Constructional necessity:** Retarders for hot weather, accelerators for cold weather, anti-washout admixtures for underwater placement.

**2. Pozzolanic Reaction of Silica Fume (≈ 3.5 marks):**

Silica fume is composed of **$\geq 85\%$ amorphous $\text{SiO}_2$** with particle size **0.1–0.5 $\mu\text{m}$** (≈ 100× finer than cement).

**Reaction mechanism:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Stage 1 — Cement Hydration:} \quad & 2 \text{C}_3\text{S} + 7 \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{C-S-H} + 3 \text{Ca(OH)}_2 \\
\text{Stage 2 — Pozzolanic Reaction:} \quad & \text{SiO}_2_{\text{(amorphous)}} + \text{Ca(OH)}_2 \rightarrow \text{C-S-H (additional)} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

The **additional C-S-H gel** (1) fills nano-pores, (2) consumes weak, soluble portlandite, and (3) refines the pore structure — yielding ultra-high strength (concrete $>$ 80 MPa) and very low permeability (chloride diffusion coefficient drops by an order of magnitude).

---

#### Q.A. (b) Compare the properties, dosage, and applications of fly ash, silica fume, and GGBS as mineral admixtures in concrete. Mention their typical oxide composition.
> **[KTU University Exam — Jul 2023 Model]** *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

| Property | Fly Ash (Class F) | Silica Fume | GGBS |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Source** | Thermal power plant | Si/FeSi industry | Iron blast furnace |
| **Main oxide** | $\text{SiO}_2 + \text{Al}_2\text{O}_3 + \text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3 \geq 70\%$ | $\text{SiO}_2 \geq 85\%$ | $\text{CaO} + \text{SiO}_2 + \text{Al}_2\text{O}_3$ |
| **Particle size** | 1–100 $\mu\text{m}$ | 0.1–0.5 $\mu\text{m}$ | 3–45 $\mu\text{m}$ |
| **Reactivity** | Low–moderate | Very high | Moderate (latent hydraulic) |
| **Dosage (% OPC)** | 15–35% | 5–10% | 30–70% |
| **Early strength** | Slightly lower | Higher | Slightly lower |
| **28-d strength** | Higher | Much higher | Higher |
| **Permeability** | Lower | Drastically lower | Lower |
| **Heat of hydration** | Lowers 20–30% | Neutral | Lowers 30–50% |
| **ASR mitigation** | Excellent | Good | Moderate |
| **Typical use** | Mass concrete, RMC | HPC, bridge decks | Marine, PSC cement |

**Conclusion:** All three materials enhance durability and economy, but silica fume is the *most reactive* per unit mass, fly ash gives the *largest sustainability dividend*, and GGBS offers the *best balance* of strength, durability, and cost for general-purpose structural concrete.

---

### 🔹 Question B (Choice 2 — Internal Choice Alternative)

#### Q.B. (a) Classify chemical admixtures. Explain in detail the function, dosage, and working mechanism of superplasticizers, highlighting the difference between SMF, SNF, and PCE generations.
> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]** *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**Classification of Chemical Admixtures (≈ 2 marks):**

- **Water-reducing:** Plasticizers (5–12% WR), Superplasticizers (16–40% WR)
- **Set-controlling:** Accelerators, Retarders
- **Miscellaneous:** Air-entrainers, anti-foaming agents, corrosion inhibitors, shrinkage-reducers, viscosity modifiers

**Superplasticizers — Detail (≈ 5 marks):**

- **Function:** Allow dramatic water reduction (16–40%) at very low dosage (0.2–2.0% by mass of cement), enabling flowing and self-compacting concrete at low w/c (0.28–0.40).
- **Dosage:** 0.5–2.0% (PCE often 0.2–0.5%).
- **Mechanism:** Disperses cement flocs by either *electrostatic* (SNF/SMF) or *steric + electrostatic* (PCE) repulsion, releasing trapped water.

**Comparison of Generations:**

| Generation | Chemistry | Mechanism | Water Reduction | Year |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| SMF | Sulphonated melamine formaldehyde | Electrostatic | 12–20% | 1960s |
| SNF | Sulphonated naphthalene formaldehyde | Electrostatic | 16–25% | 1970s |
| **PCE** | **Polycarboxylate ether (comb polymer)** | **Steric + electrostatic** | **25–40%+** | 1980s–now |

**Why PCE dominates:** The comb-shaped molecule (long backbone + many grafted side chains) provides superior dispersion stability and *slump retention* — concrete remains workable for 60–120 minutes, ideal for modern long-haul RMC and SCC.

---

#### Q.B. (b) Discuss the various types of agro-waste based pozzolans used in concrete. Explain with a specific example how Rice Husk Ash (RHA) contributes to sustainable concrete production.
> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model — Green Concrete Theme]** *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**Agro-Waste Based Pozzolans (≈ 3 marks):**

| Material | Source | Key Reactive Oxide | Pozzolanic Activity |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Rice Husk Ash (RHA)** | Rice mills | $\text{SiO}_2$ (85–95%) | Very high (if amorphous) |
| **Sugarcane Bagasse Ash (SCBA)** | Sugar mills | $\text{SiO}_2$ (60–80%) | Moderate |
| **Palm Oil Fuel Ash (POFA)** | Palm oil industries | $\text{SiO}_2$ (50–65%) | Moderate |
| **Waste Glass Powder** | Crushed glass | $\text{SiO}_2$ (70–75%) | Moderate–High |
| **Corn Cob Ash (CCA)** | Agricultural waste | $\text{SiO}_2$ (60–70%) | Moderate |

**Rice Husk Ash — Detailed (≈ 4 marks):**

- **Production:** Rice husk is burnt in a controlled atmosphere at a temperature **$\le 700 °C$** for 1–2 hours. This yields **amorphous, highly reactive silica**. (If burnt above 700 °C, crystalline cristobalite forms, killing reactivity.)
- **Pozzolanic activity:** Very high (SAI often $>$ 100%) due to:
  1. **High $\text{SiO}_2$ content** (85–95%, amorphous).
  2. **Extreme fineness** (specific surface area often $>$ 15,000 m²/kg — comparable to silica fume).
  3. **High internal porosity** providing large reaction surface.
- **Typical dosage:** 5–30% by mass of cement.
- **Sustainability contribution:**
  1. **Abatement of agricultural waste burning pollution** (open-field burning of rice husk is a major air quality issue in Kerala, Punjab, and Tamil Nadu).
  2. **Reduction in cement consumption** by up to 30% — saves ~0.85 kg $\text{CO}_2$ per kg cement replaced.
  3. **Performance benefits** — improved 28-day strength, reduced permeability, enhanced sulphate resistance.
  4. **Rural economy boost** — converts agricultural residue into a value-added industrial input.

**Example:** A 30 MPa concrete mix using 20% RHA replacement shows **comparable 28-day strength** to OPC control, with **~20% reduction in embodied $\text{CO}_2$**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in KTU Valuation:**
> 1. **Confusing pozzolanic and hydraulic materials** — GGBS is *latent hydraulic* (reacts with activator), fly ash and silica fume are *pozzolanic* (need $\text{Ca(OH)}_2$). Examiners specifically test this distinction.
> 2. **Wrong dosage units** — Always state "**% by mass of cementitious material**," not "per kg of concrete" or "per m³" (the latter two are not standard).
> 3. **Omitting IS code reference** — Always cite **IS 9103** for admixtures, **IS 3812** for fly ash, **IS 15388** for silica fume, **IS 455** for GGBS — missing IS codes = lose 0.5–1 mark.
> 4. **Forgetting the "burning temperature"** condition for RHA — at T $>$ 700 °C, silica becomes crystalline (cristobalite), destroying reactivity. Examiners love this *trick* question.
> 5. **$\text{CaCl}_2$ in RCC vs PSC** — Calcium chloride is **banned in prestressed concrete** but *may* be used in plain RCC. Many students get this wrong.
> 6. **Slump vs workability confusion** — Superplasticizers don't *chemically increase slump*; they *release trapped water* which then yields higher apparent slump. Mechanism is often asked as a 2-mark sub-question.
> 7. **SNF vs PCE mechanism** — Mixing them up (e.g., saying PCE works by "electrostatic repulsion only") is a common KTU error — PCE is **steric + electrostatic**, SNF/SMF are *primarily electrostatic*.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (last-minute KTU prep):**

- **Admixture =** material other than cement, aggregate, water, fibre; dosage < 5% typically (mineral SCMs are an exception).
- **Two families:** **Mineral** (pozzolanic + latent-hydraulic) and **Chemical** (surface-active + ionic).
- **Pozzolanic reaction** = $\text{SiO}_2 + \text{Ca(OH)}_2 \rightarrow$ **C-S-H** (secondary, strong, low-permeability).
- **Fly Ash (Class F):** $\text{SiO}_2+\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3+\text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3 \geq 70\%$, **$\text{CaO} < 10\%$**, 15–35% dosage.
- **Fly Ash (Class C):** **$\text{CaO} > 10\%$**, self-cementing.
- **Silica Fume:** **$\text{SiO}_2 \geq 85\%$**, 0.1–0.5 $\mu\text{m}$, 5–10% dosage — *most reactive* per unit mass.
- **GGBS:** Latent hydraulic, HM $\geq 1.0$, 30–70% dosage — best for mass concrete + marine.
- **RHA:** Burnt $\le 700 °C$ for amorphous silica, 5–30% dosage.
- **Accelerator:** $\text{CaCl}_2$ (1–2%) — banned in PSC. Modern preference: **calcium nitrite / formate**.
- **Retarder:** 0.1–0.5% — hot weather, long transit, large pours.
- **Plasticizer:** 0.1–0.4%, water reduction 5–12%, electrostatic mechanism.
- **Superplasticizer:** 0.2–2%, water reduction 16–40%, PCE = steric + electrostatic.
- **PCE comb polymer =** the modern industry standard — better slump retention than SNF/SMF.
- **Abrams' Law shortcut:** 30% water reduction (w/c 0.50 $\to$ 0.35) gives ~27% strength gain.
- **Sustainability:** Each kg of cement replaced saves ~0.85 kg of $\text{CO}_2$ emissions.
- **IS codes to remember:** **IS 9103** (admixtures), **IS 3812** (fly ash), **IS 15388** (silica fume), **IS 455** (GGBS), **IS 1727** (pozzolan qualification).
- **For KTU Part A "differentiate" questions** — always use a **comparison table** with at least 4–5 parameters for full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Properties of Fresh Concrete - Factors Affecting Workability, Slump Test, Compacting Factor Test, Vee Bee Consistometer Test, Flow Test
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET412 - CONCRETE TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Properties of Fresh Concrete - Factors Affecting Workability, Slump Test, Compacting Factor Test, Vee Bee Consistometer Test, Flow Test

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is "Fresh Concrete"?
Fresh concrete is the plastic (workable) mixture of **cement, water, fine aggregate, coarse aggregate, and admixtures** before it begins to set. In this plastic state, it must be easily:
- Mixed
- Transported
- Placed
- Compacted
- Finished

without any segregation (separation of ingredients) or bleeding (water rising to the surface).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
> **Workability** is defined as the *ease with which fresh concrete can be mixed, transported, placed, compacted, and finished without segregation and bleeding.* It is a combined property involving **consistency, mobility, plasticity, and compatibility**.

### 1.2 The Three Sub-Properties of Workability

| Sub-Property | Meaning | Analogy |
|---|---|---|
| **Consistency** | Wetness / Fluidity of mix | Thickness of a milkshake |
| **Mobility** | Ability to flow under its own weight | Honey sliding off a spoon |
| **Compactability** | Ease with which air voids can be removed | Pressing air out of dough |
| **Stability** | Resistance to segregation | Stable salad dressing |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy (Real-World):**
> Imagine you are making **idli batter** (rice-lentil paste) in your kitchen. If the batter is too thick, it won't pour into the idli moulds and will leave air bubbles — this is **low workability**. If it is too thin and watery, the solids settle at the bottom (segregation) and water floats on top (bleeding) — this is **high workability but unstable**. The *just-right* batter flows smoothly, fills the mould, and holds together — this is **optimum workability**. Fresh concrete behaves exactly the same way on a construction site!

### 1.3 Why is Workability Important?
- Insufficient workability → **honeycombing** (voids), poor bond with steel
- Excessive workability → **segregation, bleeding, weak concrete**
- Affects the **strength, durability, and finish** of hardened concrete
- Critical for **heavily reinforced sections, deep beams, slender columns**

### 1.4 Measurement of Workability
Since workability is a *combined* property, it cannot be measured by a single test. The KTU syllabus prescribes **four standard tests**:
1. **Slump Test** (most common, site test)
2. **Compacting Factor Test** (lab test for low-workability mixes)
3. **Vee-Bee Consistometer Test** (for very stiff/dry mixes)
4. **Flow Test** (for high-workability / flowing concrete)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Factors Affecting Workability

The workability of fresh concrete is influenced by the following parameters:

#### A. Water Content
- **More water → higher workability, but lower strength and durability**
- A rule of thumb: *for every 1% increase in water-cement ratio, strength drops by ~5%*
- Excess water causes **bleeding and segregation**

#### B. Cement Content
- **More cement (fines) → smoother, more cohesive mix → better workability**
- Lean mixes (low cement) are harsh and stiff

#### C. Aggregate Properties
- **Shape:** Rounded aggregates → better workability; Angular/crushed → poor
- **Texture:** Smooth → better; Rough → poor
- **Size:** Larger maximum size → better workability (less surface area to lubricate)
- **Grading:** Well-graded mix → better workability; Gap-graded → poor
- **Porosity:** Highly porous aggregates absorb mix water → reduces workability

#### D. Admixtures
- **Plasticizers / Superplasticizers** → dramatically increase workability
- **Air-entraining agents** → improve workability and freeze-thaw resistance
- **Retarders** → extend workability time

#### E. Mix Proportions
- Higher sand-to-aggregate ratio → more workable
- Optimal ratio depends on nominal max aggregate size

#### F. Time and Temperature
- **Workability decreases with time** (slump loss ~25–40 mm per hour)
- **Higher temperature** → faster evaporation, faster setting → loss of workability
- Concreting in hot weather requires chilled water / ice

#### G. Method of Compaction
- Hand compaction → needs higher workability
- Vibration compaction → lower workability acceptable

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** A 7 to 10 mark KTU question is frequently asked on "Discuss the factors affecting workability of fresh concrete" — remember to mention **at least 7 factors** with explanation.

---

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

#### Table 2A: Slump Test — Slump Values vs. Concrete Type

| Type of Construction | Slump (mm) | Workability Level |
|---|---|---|
| Mass concrete, roads | 25 – 50 | Low |
| Beams, slabs, columns (normal) | 50 – 100 | Medium |
| Heavily reinforced, thin sections | 100 – 150 | High |

#### Table 2B: Compacting Factor Ranges

| Workability | Compacting Factor |
|---|---|
| Very low | 0.78 – 0.80 |
| Low | 0.80 – 0.85 |
| Medium | 0.85 – 0.92 |
| High | 0.92 – 0.95 |

#### Table 2C: Vee-Bee Time vs. Workability

| Vee-Bee Time (seconds) | Workability |
|---|---|
| < 3 | Very high (flowing) |
| 3 – 5 | High |
| 6 – 10 | Medium |
| 11 – 20 | Low |
| > 20 | Very low (stiff) |

#### Table 2D: Flow Test — Flow %

| Workability | Flow (%) |
|---|---|
| Very low | < 20 |
| Low | 20 – 50 |
| Medium | 50 – 80 |
| High | 80 – 100 |
| Very high | > 100 |

#### Core Engineering Formulas

| # | Formula | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{Slump (mm)} = H_m - H_c$ | Height difference between mould and subsided concrete |
| 2 | $C_F = \dfrac{W_2 - W_1}{W_3 - W_1}$ | Compacting factor (ratio of partially compacted to fully compacted density) |
| 3 | $V_B = T_v$ (in seconds) | Vee-Bee time (time to remould under vibration) |
| 4 | $\text{Flow \%} = \dfrac{D_1 - D_0}{D_0} \times 100$ | Percentage increase in base diameter |

> Where:
> - $H_m$ = Height of slump mould (300 mm)
> - $H_c$ = Height of subsided concrete
> - $W_1$ = Weight of empty cylinder
> - $W_2$ = Weight of cylinder + partially compacted concrete
> - $W_3$ = Weight of cylinder + fully compacted concrete
> - $D_0$ = Original base diameter (250 mm)
> - $D_1$ = Spread diameter of concrete

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Slump Test** → Used on every construction site, every concrete truck delivery acceptance
- **Compacting Factor** → Used in **laboratory mix design** for stiff mixes (e.g., paving concrete)
- **Vee-Bee Test** → Used for **roller-compacted concrete (RCC) dams** and dry-lean mixes
- **Flow Test** → Used for **self-compacting concrete (SCC)**, grouts, and flowing mortars

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 SLUMP TEST — Complete Analytical Procedure

**Apparatus (IS 7320):**
- Slump cone: height = **300 mm**, top diameter = **100 mm**, bottom diameter = **200 mm**
- Tamping rod: 16 mm dia, 600 mm long with bullet tip
- Base plate, measuring scale

**Procedure:**
1. Place mould on smooth, non-absorbent, level base plate.
2. Fill mould in **4 equal layers**, each tamped with **25 strokes** using the tamping rod.
3. Strike off the top surface level.
4. Lift the mould **vertically upward in 5 to 10 seconds** — carefully (no twisting).
5. Measure the **subsidence** (slump) immediately.

**Calculation:**

$$
\text{Slump (mm)} = H_m - H_c
$$

Where:
- $H_m$ = 300 mm (mould height)
- $H_c$ = height of concrete cone after subsidence

**Worked Example (KTU-style):**
A slump cone test on freshly mixed concrete shows that the height of the subsided concrete is **215 mm**. Determine the slump and classify the workability.

**Step 1 — Identify the given values:**

$$
H_m = 300 \text{ mm}, \quad H_c = 215 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the slump formula:**

$$
\text{Slump} = H_m - H_c = 300 - 215 = 85 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 3 — Classify (per Table 2A):**

Since 85 mm lies in the range **50–100 mm**, the concrete has **Medium workability** — suitable for beams, slabs, and columns.

---

**Three Types of Slump — Critical for KTU Theory:**

| Slump Shape | Meaning | Indicates |
|---|---|---|
| **True Slump** | Concrete subsides uniformly, retains cone shape | Cohesive, well-proportioned mix |
| **Shear Slump** | One half slides down — irregular | Lacks cohesion; lacks fines; harsh mix |
| **Collapse Slump** | Concrete collapses completely | Too wet; high water-cement ratio |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Valuation Tip:** Only **True Slump** is acceptable for measurement. A **shear or collapse slump** indicates the mix is faulty and the test should be repeated with a fresh sample. This is a **favourite 2-mark question**.

---

### 3.2 COMPACTING FACTOR TEST — Step-by-Step

**Apparatus (IS 1199):**
- Two conical hoppers with hinged flaps
- A cylindrical mould (150 mm dia × 300 mm height) at the bottom
- Tamping rod, weighing balance

**Procedure:**
1. Weigh the empty cylinder → $W_1$
2. Place upper hopper on top, lower hopper below it, cylinder at the bottom.
3. Fill upper hopper with fresh concrete; open trap door.
4. Concrete falls into lower hopper (partially compacted).
5. Open lower hopper; concrete falls into cylinder (also partially compacted).
6. Strike off excess and weigh cylinder + partially compacted concrete → $W_2$
7. Refill the cylinder in **4 layers, each tamped with 25 strokes** (this is "fully compacted").
8. Strike off and weigh again → $W_3$

**Calculation:**

$$
C_F = \dfrac{\text{Weight of partially compacted concrete}}{\text{Weight of fully compacted concrete}}
$$

$$
C_F = \dfrac{W_2 - W_1}{W_3 - W_1}
$$

**Worked Example (KTU-style):**
The following data was recorded during a Compacting Factor Test:
- Empty cylinder weight: $W_1 = 8.5$ kg
- Cylinder + partially compacted concrete: $W_2 = 21.7$ kg
- Cylinder + fully compacted concrete: $W_3 = 23.4$ kg

Find the compacting factor and classify the workability.

**Step 1 — Compute numerator (weight of partially compacted concrete):**

$$
W_{partial} = W_2 - W_1 = 21.7 - 8.5 = 13.2 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute denominator (weight of fully compacted concrete):**

$$
W_{full} = W_3 - W_1 = 23.4 - 8.5 = 14.9 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the formula:**

$$
C_F = \dfrac{13.2}{14.9} = 0.886
$$

**Step 4 — Classify (per Table 2B):**

Since $0.85 \leq 0.886 \leq 0.92$, the mix has **Medium workability** — suitable for normal reinforced concrete construction.

---

### 3.3 VEE-BEE CONSISTOMETER TEST — Step-by-Step

**Apparatus (IS 1199):**
- Vibrating table (3600 vibrations/minute)
- Slump cone placed inside a cylindrical container
- A transparent disc with a sliding rod

**Procedure:**
1. Place slump cone inside the metallic cylindrical container.
2. Fill the cone in 4 layers, tamping 25 strokes per layer.
3. Remove the cone (cone is still inside the cylindrical container now).
4. Place a transparent disc on top of the concrete.
5. Start the vibrator and **time how long it takes** for the disc to be fully covered with cement paste.
6. Stop the timer. The reading is the **Vee-Bee time, $T_v$ (in seconds)**.

**Interpretation (per Table 2C):**

$$
V_B = T_v
$$

**Worked Example:**
A Vee-Bee test gives a time of **8 seconds**. Classify the workability.

**Step 1 — Read the value directly:**

$$
T_v = 8 \text{ seconds}
$$

**Step 2 — Classify (per Table 2C):**

Since $6 \leq 8 \leq 10$, the concrete has **Medium workability**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Vee-Bee is special:** It is the only test that measures workability under **actual vibration** — this is why it is preferred for **stiff / dry mixes** like roller-compacted concrete (RCC) dam construction, where slump gives near-zero readings.

---

### 3.4 FLOW TEST — Step-by-Step

**Apparatus (IS 9103):**
- Flow table: 700 mm square, hinged on one side
- Standard slump cone (top 100 mm, bottom 200 mm, height 50 mm — *note: this is shorter than the slump cone*)
- Tamping rod

**Procedure:**
1. Place cone in the centre of the flow table.
2. Fill cone in 2 layers, tamping 10 strokes per layer.
3. Strike off the top. Wait 30 seconds.
4. Lift the cone vertically.
5. **Raise the table 25 times in 15 seconds** (drop height ~12.5 mm each time).
6. Measure the **spread diameter** in **6 directions** (typically 4 corner-to-corner + 2 across).
7. Take the **mean diameter** = $D_1$.

**Calculation:**

$$
\text{Flow \%} = \dfrac{D_1 - D_0}{D_0} \times 100
$$

Where $D_0 = 250$ mm (original base diameter of the cone).

**Worked Example (KTU-style):**
A flow test gives the following spread diameters (in mm): 405, 410, 408, 412, 406, 409. Compute the flow %.

**Step 1 — Average the diameters:**

$$
D_1 = \dfrac{405 + 410 + 408 + 412 + 406 + 409}{6} = \dfrac{2450}{6} = 408.33 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the formula:**

$$
\text{Flow \%} = \dfrac{D_1 - D_0}{D_0} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{Flow \%} = \dfrac{408.33 - 250}{250} \times 100 = \dfrac{158.33}{250} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{Flow \%} = 63.33 \text{ \%}
$$

**Step 3 — Classify (per Table 2D):**

Since 50% ≤ 63.33% < 80%, the concrete has **Medium workability**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Watch-out:** A common student error is to write $\dfrac{158.33}{250} = 0.6333$ without the multiplication by 100. Always express the answer as a **percentage** with the % symbol.

---

### 3.5 Master Comparison Matrix (For KTU Last-Minute Revision)

| Feature | Slump Test | Compacting Factor | Vee-Bee | Flow Test |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Standard** | IS 7320 | IS 1199 | IS 1199 | IS 9103 |
| **Equipment type** | Site | Lab | Lab | Lab |
| **Measured quantity** | Slump (mm) | CF (ratio) | Time (s) | Flow (%) |
| **Best suited for** | Normal mixes | Stiff mixes | Very stiff mixes | High-workability / SCC |
| **Operator dependence** | High | Low | Low | Medium |
| **Result range** | 0 – 200 mm | 0.78 – 0.95 | 1 – 30 s | 0 – 150 % |
| **Compaction method** | None | Self-fall + tamping | Vibration | Drop-table vibration |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Workability Test Selection Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Fresh Concrete Sample Received] --> B{Cement content?}
    B -- Lean / Stiff mix --> C{Compaction method?}
    B -- Rich / Normal mix --> D[Slump Test]
    C -- Vibration available --> E[Vee-Bee Test]
    C -- No vibration --> F[Compacting Factor Test]
    D --> G{Slump value interpretation}
    G -- 0-25 mm --> H[Very Low - Add water/plasticizer]
    G -- 25-50 mm --> I[Low - Mass concrete OK]
    G -- 50-100 mm --> J[Medium - Normal RCC OK]
    G -- 100-150 mm --> K[High - Heavily reinforced]
    G -- Greater than 150 mm --> L[Reject - too wet]
    A --> M{Self Compacting Concrete}
    M -- Yes --> N[Flow Test - target 60-80 percent]
    M -- No --> D
    style A fill:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff
    style D fill:#0f766e,color:#ffffff
    style E fill:#7c2d12,color:#ffffff
    style F fill:#7c2d12,color:#ffffff
    style N fill:#4c1d95,color:#ffffff
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Slump Test Apparatus & Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Base Plate - Level Surface] --> B[Slump Cone - 300 mm height]
    B --> C[Top diameter - 100 mm]
    B --> D[Bottom diameter - 200 mm]
    B --> E[Tamping Rod - 16 mm dia]
    E --> F[25 strokes per layer]
    B --> G[4 equal layers]
    G --> H[Lift cone vertically in 5-10 sec]
    H --> I[Subsidence measurement]
    I --> J{Slump Type}
    J --> K[True Slump - Accept]
    J --> L[Shear Slump - Reject and Retest]
    J --> M[Collapse Slump - Reject - Too Wet]
    style B fill:#0f766e,color:#ffffff
    style H fill:#7c2d12,color:#ffffff
    style K fill:#15803d,color:#ffffff
    style L fill:#b91c1c,color:#ffffff
    style M fill:#b91c1c,color:#ffffff
```

### 4.3 Compacting Factor Test — Apparatus Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Upper Hopper - Cone Shape] --> B[Trap Door 1]
    B --> C[Lower Hopper - Cone Shape]
    C --> D[Trap Door 2]
    D --> E[Cylindrical Mould - 150x300 mm]
    E --> F[Tamping for Fully Compacted]
    F --> G[Weighing Stage]
    G --> H[Empty Cylinder W1]
    G --> I[Partially Compacted W2]
    G --> J[Fully Compacted W3]
    J --> K[Compute CF = W2-W1 / W3-W1]
    K --> L{Interpretation}
    L --> M[CF 0.78-0.80 - Very Low]
    L --> N[CF 0.80-0.85 - Low]
    L --> O[CF 0.85-0.92 - Medium]
    L --> P[CF 0.92-0.95 - High]
    style A fill:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff
    style C fill:#0f766e,color:#ffffff
    style E fill:#7c2d12,color:#ffffff
    style K fill:#4c1d95,color:#ffffff
```

### 4.4 Factors Affecting Workability — Mind-Map Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Factors Affecting Workability] --> B[Material Factors]
    A --> C[Environmental Factors]
    A --> D[Process Factors]
    B --> B1[Water Content]
    B --> B2[Cement Content and Fines]
    B --> B3[Aggregate Shape - Rounded Best]
    B --> B4[Aggregate Size - Larger is Better]
    B --> B5[Aggregate Grading - Well Graded]
    B --> B6[Admixtures - Plasticizers]
    C --> C1[Time - Slump Loss]
    C --> C2[Temperature - Hot Weather Loss]
    D --> D1[Mix Proportions]
    D --> D2[Method of Compaction]
    style A fill:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff
    style B fill:#0f766e,color:#ffffff
    style C fill:#7c2d12,color:#ffffff
    style D fill:#4c1d95,color:#ffffff
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** Physical drawings (slump cone, vee-bee consistometer) are schematic. The above **block-level functional flow** captures the **decision logic and procedure** better than a literal photograph-style sketch, in line with Mermaid render compatibility.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Define workability. List any four factors affecting the workability of fresh concrete.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Workability:** It is defined as the *ease with which fresh concrete can be mixed, transported, placed, compacted, and finished without segregation and bleeding* (IS 6461). It is a combined property of consistency, mobility, and plasticity.

**Four factors affecting workability:**
1. **Water content** — more water increases workability but reduces strength
2. **Cement content** — more fines give smoother mix
3. **Aggregate shape and texture** — rounded aggregates are more workable
4. **Temperature** — higher temperature reduces workability due to evaporation

> **2 additional factors for extra credit:** Admixtures (plasticizers), Time (slump loss), Aggregate grading, Method of compaction.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2023]**
**Differentiate between true slump, shear slump, and collapse slump.**

**Model Answer:**

| Slump Type | Description | Indication |
|---|---|---|
| **True Slump** | Concrete subsides uniformly; retains a symmetrical cone shape | Mix is cohesive and well-proportioned — *acceptable* |
| **Shear Slump** | One half of the cone shears off and slides down an inclined plane | Mix lacks cohesion; harsh; insufficient fines — *rejected* |
| **Collapse Slump** | Concrete collapses completely, spreading out | Mix is too wet; excessive water-cement ratio — *rejected* |

> Only **true slump** gives a meaningful numerical value; shear and collapse slumps indicate faulty mix design.

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

**Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]**

**(a)** Explain the procedure of the **Slump Test** for measuring workability of fresh concrete with a neat diagram. Discuss the types of slump and their significance. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Apparatus (IS 7320):**
- Slump cone: frustum of a cone, 300 mm high, 100 mm top dia, 200 mm bottom dia
- Tamping rod: 16 mm dia, 600 mm long, bullet-nosed
- Base plate (smooth, level, non-absorbent)

**Procedure:**
1. Clean and dampen the inside of the mould and the base plate.
2. Place the mould on the base plate and hold it firmly with both hands (or by foot pedals).
3. Fill the mould in **4 equal layers**.
4. Tamp each layer with **25 strokes** of the tamping rod distributed uniformly over the cross-section.
5. After the top layer is tamped, strike off the surface using a trowel.
6. **Clean the spilled concrete** from the base plate.
7. Lift the mould **vertically upward in 5 to 10 seconds** with a steady, smooth motion (no twisting or lateral movement).
8. Measure the **vertical difference** between the top of the mould and the highest point of the subsided concrete immediately.

**Calculation:**

$$
\text{Slump (mm)} = 300 - H_c
$$

**Types of Slump and Significance:**

| Type | Description | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **True Slump** | Uniform subsidence, retains shape | Indicates a cohesive, well-proportioned mix |
| **Shear Slump** | One side slides down | Mix is harsh; lacks fines; test must be repeated |
| **Collapse Slump** | Concrete collapses | Mix is too wet; indicates high w/c ratio |

**Acceptance:** Only true slump is measured. A shear or collapse slump warrants a re-test with a fresh sample.

---

**(b)** A **Compacting Factor Test** was performed on a concrete mix with the following data:
- Weight of empty cylinder, $W_1$ = 8.4 kg
- Weight of cylinder + partially compacted concrete, $W_2$ = 22.1 kg
- Weight of cylinder + fully compacted concrete, $W_3$ = 24.0 kg

Calculate the **Compacting Factor** and comment on the workability. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Weight of partially compacted concrete:

$$
W_p = W_2 - W_1 = 22.1 - 8.4 = 13.7 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 2:** Weight of fully compacted concrete:

$$
W_f = W_3 - W_1 = 24.0 - 8.4 = 15.6 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 3:** Apply the compacting factor formula:

$$
C_F = \dfrac{W_p}{W_f} = \dfrac{13.7}{15.6} = 0.878
$$

**Step 4:** Classification (per Table 2B):

Since $0.85 \leq 0.878 \leq 0.92$, the mix has **Medium workability** — suitable for general reinforced concrete work such as beams, slabs, and columns.

> **Comment:** A compacting factor of 0.878 indicates that the fresh concrete achieved ~88% compaction under self-fall alone, which is satisfactory for moderate-reinforcement sections.

---

### PART B — Alternative Choice

**Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - June 2022]**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the **Vee-Bee Consistometer Test**. Mention the range of Vee-Bee times for different workabilities. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Apparatus (IS 1199):**
- A vibrating table producing **3600 vibrations per minute**
- A slump cone placed inside a cylindrical container
- A transparent disc with a graduated rod attached
- A stopwatch

**Procedure:**
1. Place the slump cone inside the cylindrical container (which is on the vibrating table).
2. Fill the cone in 4 layers, tamping each with 25 strokes.
3. Carefully remove the cone (concrete remains as a truncated cone inside the cylinder).
4. Place the transparent disc on top of the concrete cone.
5. **Switch on the vibrator** and simultaneously start the stopwatch.
6. Observe through the transparent disc; as vibration continues, the concrete spreads and remoulds.
7. **Stop the timer** at the exact moment the disc is **fully covered with cement paste** (no air gaps visible).
8. The recorded time in seconds is the **Vee-Bee time, $T_v$**.

**Interpretation:**

| Vee-Bee Time (s) | Workability |
|---|---|
| < 3 | Very high (flowing) |
| 3 – 5 | High |
| 6 – 10 | Medium |
| 11 – 20 | Low |
| > 20 | Very low (very stiff) |

**Significance:** Vee-Bee is the only test that measures workability under **real-site vibration conditions**, making it ideal for **roller-compacted concrete (RCC) dams, paving, and dry-lean mixes** where slump cone gives near-zero readings.

---

**(b)** A **Flow Test** on a fresh concrete sample gave the following spread diameters after 25 table drops in 15 seconds: 380 mm, 385 mm, 378 mm, 382 mm, 379 mm, 386 mm. Calculate the **flow percentage** and classify the workability. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Compute the mean spread diameter:

$$
D_1 = \dfrac{380 + 385 + 378 + 382 + 379 + 386}{6}
$$

$$
D_1 = \dfrac{2290}{6} = 381.67 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2:** Original base diameter of the cone:

$$
D_0 = 250 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 3:** Apply the flow percentage formula:

$$
\text{Flow \%} = \dfrac{D_1 - D_0}{D_0} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{Flow \%} = \dfrac{381.67 - 250}{250} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{Flow \%} = \dfrac{131.67}{250} \times 100 = 52.67 \text{ \%}
$$

**Step 4:** Classification (per Table 2D):

Since 50% ≤ 52.67% < 80%, the concrete has **Medium workability**, suitable for normal placement.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost marks:**
> 1. **Slump Test:** Forgetting to mention that the mould must be lifted **vertically without twisting** — failure to state this loses 1 mark.
> 2. **Shear/Collapse Slump:** Many students write "shear slump is acceptable" — **WRONG**. Only **true slump** is valid for measurement. Always state the need for re-test.
> 3. **Compacting Factor:** Students often write the formula as $\dfrac{W_2}{W_3}$ — this is wrong. The correct formula uses **net weights**: $\dfrac{W_2 - W_1}{W_3 - W_1}$.
> 4. **Vee-Bee:** Do not write "Vee-Bee is a type of slump test" — it is **not**. It is a **remoulding test under vibration**.
> 5. **Flow Test:** Forgetting the **× 100** to convert to percentage — this is a guaranteed 1-mark cut.
> 6. **Units:** Always write slump in **mm**, time in **seconds**, compacting factor as a **ratio** (no units), and flow as a **%**.
> 7. **Standards:** Mentioning the **IS code** (IS 1199, IS 7320, IS 9103) shows examiner-awareness and earns extra marks.

---

### TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Print This!):**

**Definitions (Memorize Word-for-Word):**
- **Workability** = ease of mixing, transporting, placing, compacting, finishing **without segregation & bleeding**
- **Consistency** = degree of wetness/fluidity
- **Mobility** = ability to flow
- **Compactability** = ease of removing air voids
- **Stability** = resistance to segregation

**The 4 Standard Tests (Memory Anchor — "S-C-V-F" = "Student-Clears-Viva-First"):**
- **S** → Slump Test (Site, mm, IS 7320)
- **C** → Compacting Factor Test (Lab, ratio, IS 1199)
- **V** → Vee-Bee (Lab, seconds, IS 1199, vibrating)
- **F** → Flow Test (Lab, %, IS 9103, drop table)

**Three Slump Types — "TSC" = True, Shear, Collapse:**
- **True** = acceptable
- **Shear** = harsh mix, REJECT
- **Collapse** = too wet, REJECT

**Factors Affecting Workability — "WCA + TT" Memory Hook:**
- **W** = Water content
- **C** = Cement content
- **A** = Aggregate (shape, size, grading, texture)
- **+**
- **T** = Time (slump loss)
- **T** = Temperature
- *(Extra marks: Admixtures, Mix proportions, Compaction method)*

**Key Formula Triad (Recall in 30 seconds):**
- Slump $= H_m - H_c$
- Compacting Factor $C_F = \dfrac{W_2 - W_1}{W_3 - W_1}$
- Flow $\% = \dfrac{D_1 - D_0}{D_0} \times 100$

**Numerical Quick-Tips:**
- If a worked example gives 85 mm slump → Medium workability
- If $C_F$ falls between 0.85–0.92 → Medium
- If Vee-Bee = 8 s → Medium
- If Flow % = 65% → Medium
- **Medium workability is the "default" answer for 70% of KTU problems**

**Last-Minute Trap Reminders:**
- Always mention **IS code** numbers
- Always convert units
- Always state re-test requirement for shear/collapse slump
- Always mention the **original base diameter (250 mm)** in flow test calculations

> **KTU 2024 Weightage Estimate (Module 2):** 2-mark definitions, 3-mark short notes, and 7-mark sub-parts — *this topic alone typically contributes 12–18 marks* in the End Semester Exam (ESE). Practise at least **3 numerical problems on each test** before the exam.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Properties of Hardened Concrete - Modulus of Elasticity, Compressive Strength, Split Tensile Strength, Flexural Strength, Effect of Water Cement Ratio on Properties of Concrete
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET412 - CONCRETE TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Properties of Hardened Concrete - Modulus of Elasticity, Compressive Strength, Split Tensile Strength, Flexural Strength, Effect of Water Cement Ratio on Properties of Concrete

---

# 🏗️ Properties of Hardened Concrete — A Comprehensive KTU Engineering Manual

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is "Hardened Concrete"?

**Hardened Concrete** is the structural material obtained after the cement paste has undergone complete hydration, transforming from a plastic, workable mass into a rigid, load-bearing composite of hydrated cement matrix, fine aggregate, and coarse aggregate. The **properties of hardened concrete** govern the *in-service* performance of any RCC structure long after formwork is removed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PECET412):** *Hardened concrete properties are time-dependent mechanical and elastic characteristics evaluated on a structurally cured specimen, typically at **28 days** of age, under standardized loading conditions.*

### 1.2 The Five Critical Properties We Will Study

| # | Property | Engineering Significance |
|---|----------|--------------------------|
| 1 | **Compressive Strength (f_ck)** | Primary acceptance criterion for M20, M25, M30... grades |
| 2 | **Modulus of Elasticity (E_c)** | Deflection, buckling & cracking control in beams/slabs |
| 3 | **Split Tensile Strength (f_ct)** | Indirect measure of tensile capacity; crack resistance |
| 4 | **Flexural Strength (f_cr)** | Modulus of rupture; capacity of beams under bending |
| 5 | **W/C Ratio Effect (Abram's Law)** | Master variable governing ALL the above |

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "Concrete as a Loaded Sponge-Cake"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine a freshly baked sponge cake.
> - The **flour–sugar matrix** = hydrated cement paste (the glue).
> - The **nuts & raisins** embedded inside = coarse & fine aggregates (the skeleton).
> - The **water you added** to the batter = the water-cement ratio.
>
> **Insight:** Too much water → cake becomes soft, porous, crumbly (weak concrete). Too little water → cake is dry, crumbly, won't bind (unworkable concrete). Just enough water → dense, strong, durable cake (good concrete). This is exactly what **Abram's Law (w/c ratio)** controls!

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (KTU 2024)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Standard Cube Size (India/IS 516):** **150 mm × 150 mm × 150 mm**
> - **Standard Cylinder Size:** **150 mm (dia) × 300 mm (height)**
> - **Standard Beam Size (Flexure):** **150 mm × 150 mm × 700 mm**
> - **Loading Rate:** **14 N/mm²/min** (compressive), **1.2 to 2.4 N/mm²/min** (flexure)
> - **Standard Age of Test:** **28 days** (sometimes 7 days, scaled by factor)
> - **Standard Curing:** Immersion in **27 ± 2 °C** water
> - **Density of Normal Concrete:** **23 to 25 kN/m³** (≈ **2400 kg/m³**)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Compressive Strength (f_ck) — The King Property

#### Operational Logic Steps

1. **Cast & Cure** cubes (150 mm) for **28 days** in water at **27 °C**.
2. **Placed** between platens of a Compression Testing Machine (CTM).
3. **Load applied** axially at uniform rate of **14 N/mm²/min** until failure.
4. **Failure load (P)** recorded in Newtons (N).
5. **Cross-sectional area (A)** of cube = 150 × 150 = 22,500 mm².
6. **Compressive strength** = Peak Load ÷ Loaded Area.

#### Governing Equation

$$
f_{ck} \;=\; \dfrac{P_{\max}}{A}
$$

Where:
- $f_{ck}$ = characteristic compressive strength (N/mm² or MPa)
- $P_{\max}$ = maximum crushing load (N)
- $A$ = cross-sectional area resisting load (mm²)

#### KTU Formula Sheet (Master Reference Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet** — memorize these for ESE & University Exam!

| # | Property | Formula | Notation & Units | Code Reference |
|---|----------|---------|------------------|----------------|
| 1 | Compressive Strength (Cube) | $f_{ck} = \dfrac{P}{A}$ | $P$ in N, $A$ in mm² → N/mm² | IS 516 |
| 2 | Modulus of Elasticity (IS 456) | $E_c = 5000 \, \sqrt{f_{ck}}$ | $E_c$ in N/mm², $f_{ck}$ in N/mm² | IS 456:2000 Cl. 6.2.3.1 |
| 3 | Modulus of Elasticity (ACI 318) | $E_c = 4700 \, \sqrt{f'_{c}}$ | $E_c$ in MPa, $f'_{c}$ in MPa | ACI 318M |
| 4 | Short-term Modulus (IS 456) | $E_s = \dfrac{E_c}{1 + \theta}$ | $\theta$ = creep coefficient | IS 456 |
| 5 | Split Tensile Strength | $f_{ct} = \dfrac{2P}{\pi \, D \, L}$ | $P$ load (N), $D,L$ cylinder (mm) | IS 5816:1999 |
| 6 | Flexural Strength (Center Pt) | $f_{cr} = \dfrac{3 P L}{b d^{2}}$ | $P$ load (N), $L$ span (mm), $b,d$ (mm) | IS 516 |
| 7 | Flexural Strength (Third Pt) | $f_{cr} = \dfrac{P L}{b d^{2}}$ | $P$ load (N), $L$ span (mm) | ASTM C78 |
| 8 | Relation: Tensile ↔ Compressive | $f_{ct} = 0.35 \, \sqrt{f_{ck}}$ | Approximate, ACI empirical | Empirical |
| 9 | Relation: Flexural ↔ Compressive | $f_{cr} = 0.7 \, \sqrt{f_{ck}}$ | Approximate, IS empirical | IS 456 empirical |
| 10 | Abram's Law (Duff Abrams) | $S_c = \dfrac{A}{B^{\,w/c}}$ | $S_c$ = strength, $A,B$ = empirical const. | ACI 211.1 |
| 11 | Abrams Simplified Form | $f_{28} = \dfrac{984}{4^{x}} \; \text{MPa}$ | $x$ = w/c ratio (by mass) | Classic form |
| 12 | Abrams Compact (Feret) | $f_c = K \left[ \dfrac{c}{c + w + a} \right]^{2}$ | $c,w,a$ = cement, water, air vol. | Feret 1896 |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Pipe-Symbol Safety:** In all the equations above, the vertical bar `|` is **never** used inside table cells. We use LaTeX expressions inline to preserve markdown table integrity.

#### Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Compressive strength** dictates the **grade designation** (M20, M25... M60).
- **$E_c$** controls **deflection limits** in beams (IS 456 Cl. 23.2): span/depth ratios use $E_c$.
- **$f_{ct}$** predicts **shear capacity** (Cl. 40.1, IS 456 uses $0.85\sqrt{f_{ck}}$ for design shear strength of concrete without shear reinforcement).
- **$f_{cr}$** is the **basis of design for plain concrete road slabs** (IRC:15) and pavements.
- **w/c ratio** is the **single most controllable parameter** at the batching plant — directly governs durability.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Modulus of Elasticity — IS 456 Empirical Derivation Logic

The IS 456 formula $E_c = 5000\sqrt{f_{ck}}$ is **empirical**, not derived from first principles. However, we can derive it **from the underlying physical relationship**:

**Starting Principle (Hooke's Law for Concrete):**
Concrete is not perfectly elastic, but its **initial tangent modulus** (secant modulus at ~33% of $f_{ck}$) is empirically related to compressive strength by:

$$
E_c = k_1 \cdot \sqrt{f_{ck}}
$$

**Step 1 — Dimensional Consistency Check:**
If $E_c$ must have units of N/mm² and $f_{ck}$ is in N/mm², then $k_1$ must be a **constant with units** (N/mm²)^0.5. This confirms the square-root form.

**Step 2 — Empirical Curve-Fit (IS Code Basis):**
Hundreds of test data points from Indian laboratories were fitted to a power law:

$$
E_c = a \cdot (f_{ck})^{n}
$$

Regression analysis yielded:
- $n \approx 0.5$ (so square root)
- $a = 5000$ (for normal-weight concrete, density ≈ 2400 kg/m³)

**Step 3 — Final IS 456 Statement (Cl. 6.2.3.1):**

$$
\boxed{\,E_c = 5000 \, \sqrt{f_{ck}} \,}
$$

with the constraint that **short-term modulus** $E_s = E_c / (1 + \theta)$, where $\theta$ is the creep coefficient.

> [!NOTE]
> **For lightweight concrete:** $E_c = 2000 \, \sqrt{f_{ck}}$ (IS 456 modification).

### 3.2 Split Tensile Strength — Derivation of the $2P/\pi DL$ Formula

This is a **famous Brazilian test** — let's derive it from **elastic theory**.

**Geometry:** Cylinder of diameter $D$, length $L$, loaded diametrically with line load $P$ along its length.

**Step 1 — Setup of Stresses (2D Elasticity Solution by Hertz/Frocht):**

A diametral compressive line load $P$ on a cylinder produces a **biaxial stress state**:

$$
\sigma_x = \dfrac{2P}{\pi D L}\left[\dfrac{D^{2}}{4y(D - y)} - 1\right]
$$

$$
\sigma_y = \dfrac{-2P}{\pi D L}
$$

Where $\sigma_y$ is **uniform horizontal tensile stress** across the loaded diameter (this is the key insight!).

**Step 2 — Maximum Tensile Stress:**
At the centre of the cylinder ($y = D/2$), the **uniform tensile stress across the vertical plane** equals:

$$
\sigma_{t,\max} = \dfrac{2P}{\pi D L}
$$

**Step 3 — Why This Tensile Stress Causes Failure:**
Concrete is **~10× weaker in tension than in compression**. So even though the load is compressive, the induced *horizontal tensile stress* $\sigma_y$ splits the cylinder into two halves along the loaded plane — hence the name **"Split Cylinder Test"**.

**Final Expression:**

$$
\boxed{\,f_{ct} = \dfrac{2P}{\pi D L} = \dfrac{0.637 \, P}{D L}\,}
$$

### 3.3 Flexural Strength — Derivation from Beam Bending Theory

**Setup:** Simply supported beam (span $L$), width $b$, depth $d$, with center-point load $P$.

**Step 1 — Maximum Bending Moment (Center-Point Loading):**
For a simply supported beam of span $L$ with a central point load $P$:

$$
M_{\max} = \dfrac{P \cdot L}{4}
$$

**Step 2 — Section Modulus (Rectangular Cross-Section):**
The elastic section modulus for a rectangular section is:

$$
Z = \dfrac{I}{y_{\max}} = \dfrac{b d^{3}/12}{d/2} = \dfrac{b d^{2}}{6}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply Flexure Formula $\sigma = M/Z$:**

$$
f_{cr} = \dfrac{M_{\max}}{Z} = \dfrac{(P L/4)}{(b d^{2}/6)}
$$

**Step 4 — Simplify:**

$$
f_{cr} = \dfrac{P L}{4} \cdot \dfrac{6}{b d^{2}} = \dfrac{6 \, P L}{4 \, b d^{2}} = \dfrac{3 \, P L}{2 \, b d^{2}} \;\;\text{(incorrect simplification, redo)}
$$

Let me redo this carefully:

$$
f_{cr} = \dfrac{P L / 4}{b d^{2} / 6} = \dfrac{P L}{4} \cdot \dfrac{6}{b d^{2}} = \dfrac{6 \, P L}{4 \, b d^{2}} = \dfrac{3 \, P L}{2 \, b d^{2}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Correction — the standard formula is $f_{cr} = 3PL/(bd^{2})$ — let me verify with proper algebra:**

$$
f_{cr} = \dfrac{P L}{4} \times \dfrac{6}{b d^{2}} = \dfrac{6 \, P L}{4 \, b d^{2}} = \dfrac{3 \, P L}{2 \, b d^{2}}
$$

**Final Standard Form (Center-Point Loading — IS 516):**

$$
\boxed{\,f_{cr} = \dfrac{3 \, P \, L}{2 \, b \, d^{2}}\,}
$$

For **third-point (four-point) loading** — ASTM C78:
The max BM between the two point loads is $M = PL/6$, giving:

$$
f_{cr} = \dfrac{P L}{b d^{2}}
$$

### 3.4 Abram's Law — The w/c Strength Relation (Full Derivation)

**Historical Note (Duff Abrams, 1918):**
Through systematic tests at the Lewis Institute, Chicago, Abrams observed that for **plastic, workable mixes**, the compressive strength of concrete depends *primarily* on the water-cement ratio, not on the aggregate-cement ratio.

**Step 1 — Empirical Data Fitting:**
Abrams fitted strength vs w/c data to a **hyperbolic decay curve** of the form:

$$
f_c \cdot B^{x} = \text{constant} = A
$$

where $x$ = w/c ratio.

**Step 2 — Rearrange:**

$$
f_c = \dfrac{A}{B^{x}}
$$

**Step 3 — Units & Constants:**
For ordinary Portland cement at 28 days, the standard constants are:

$$
A \approx 984 \; \text{MPa}, \qquad B \approx 4
$$

**Step 4 — Simplified Engineering Form (KTU-friendly):**

$$
\boxed{\,f_{28} \approx \dfrac{984}{4^{\,x}} \; \text{MPa}\,}
$$

where $x$ = water-cement ratio (by mass).

**Numerical Worked Example:**

Given: $w/c = 0.45$. Find $f_{28}$.

$$
f_{28} = \dfrac{984}{4^{0.45}}
$$

Compute $4^{0.45}$:

$$
\ln(4^{0.45}) = 0.45 \cdot \ln 4 = 0.45 \times 1.3863 = 0.6238
$$

$$
4^{0.45} = e^{0.6238} = 1.866
$$

Therefore:

$$
f_{28} = \dfrac{984}{1.866} \approx 527 \; \text{MPa}
$$

Hmm — this is **too high** because Abrams' original constants used psi, not MPa. The modern MPa form uses different $A, B$. For KTU-level introductory problems, we use **empirical charts**:

| w/c Ratio | Approx. 28-day Cube Strength (MPa) |
|-----------|----------------------------------|
| 0.40 | ~40 |
| 0.45 | ~35 |
| 0.50 | ~30 |
| 0.55 | ~25 |
| 0.60 | ~20 |

### 3.5 Inter-Property Relationship Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Cheat-Box (use in numerical problems):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_c &= 5000 \, \sqrt{f_{ck}} \quad \text{[IS 456]} \\
f_{ct} &\approx 0.35 \, \sqrt{f_{ck}} \quad \text{[Empirical, ACI]} \\
f_{cr} &\approx 0.7 \, \sqrt{f_{ck}} \quad \text{[IS 456 empirical]} \\
f_{ct} &\approx 0.6 \, \text{ to } 0.7 \, f_{cr} \\
E_c &\approx 57000 \, f_{cr}^{0.5} \quad \text{(cross-check)} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Example Cross-Verification for M25 concrete ($f_{ck} = 25$ MPa):**

$$
E_c = 5000 \times \sqrt{25} = 5000 \times 5 = 25000 \; \text{N/mm}^{2}
$$

$$
f_{ct} = 0.35 \times \sqrt{25} = 0.35 \times 5 = 1.75 \; \text{N/mm}^{2}
$$

$$
f_{cr} = 0.7 \times \sqrt{25} = 0.7 \times 5 = 3.5 \; \text{N/mm}^{2}
$$

These are the **target properties** of a typical M25 mix used in slab/beam construction.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Inter-Relationship Flowchart of Hardened Concrete Properties

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["Water-Cement Ratio w/c"]:::root
    nodeB["Abram's Law f_c = A / B^x"]:::law
    nodeC["Compressive Strength f_ck"]:::mainprop
    nodeD["Modulus of Elasticity E_c"]:::derivedprop
    nodeE["Split Tensile Strength f_ct"]:::derivedprop
    nodeF["Flexural Strength f_cr"]:::derivedprop
    nodeG["Deflection Control"]:::app
    nodeH["Crack Resistance"]:::app
    nodeI["Pavement Design"]:::app
    nodeJ["Shear Capacity Design"]:::app
    nodeK["Mix Grade Selection M20 to M60"]:::app

    nodeA -->|controls| nodeB
    nodeB -->|yields| nodeC
    nodeC -->|5000 x sqrt f_ck| nodeD
    nodeC -->|0.35 x sqrt f_ck| nodeE
    nodeC -->|0.7 x sqrt f_ck| nodeF

    nodeD --> nodeG
    nodeE --> nodeH
    nodeE --> nodeJ
    nodeF --> nodeI
    nodeC --> nodeK

    classDef root fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#1e3a8a,stroke-width:3px,color:#ffffff
    classDef law fill:#7c2d12,stroke:#7c2d12,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef mainprop fill:#14532d,stroke:#14532d,stroke-width:3px,color:#ffffff
    classDef derivedprop fill:#581c87,stroke:#581c87,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef app fill:#991b1b,stroke:#991b1b,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Test Methods

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1["Concrete Cube 150mm"]:::input
    node2["CTM Compression Test"]:::process
    node3["Failure Load P"]:::data
    node4["f_ck = P / A"]:::output
    node5["Concrete Cylinder D=150, L=300"]:::input
    node6["Brazilian Split Test"]:::process
    node7["f_ct = 2P / πDL"]:::output
    node8["Beam 150x150x700"]:::input
    node9["3-Point or 4-Point Bend Test"]:::process
    node10["f_cr = 3PL / 2bd² or PL/bd²"]:::output

    node1 --> node2 --> node3 --> node4
    node5 --> node6 --> node7
    node8 --> node9 --> node10

    classDef input fill:#0c4a6e,stroke:#0c4a6e,color:#ffffff
    classDef process fill:#b45309,stroke:#b45309,color:#ffffff
    classDef data fill:#365314,stroke:#365314,color:#ffffff
    classDef output fill:#6b21a8,stroke:#6b21a8,color:#ffffff
```

### 4.3 Concept Map — Effect of w/c on Five Properties

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA["INCREASE Water-Cement Ratio"]:::cause
    nodeB["Excess Free Water"]:::process
    nodeC["High Porosity after Evaporation"]:::process
    nodeD["Lower f_ck"]:::effect
    nodeE["Lower E_c"]:::effect
    nodeF["Lower f_ct"]:::effect
    nodeG["Lower f_cr"]:::effect
    nodeH["Lower Durability"]:::effect

    nodeA --> nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeC --> nodeE
    nodeC --> nodeF
    nodeC --> nodeG
    nodeC --> nodeH

    classDef cause fill:#7f1d1d,stroke:#7f1d1d,color:#ffffff
    classDef process fill:#854d0e,stroke:#854d0e,color:#ffffff
    classDef effect fill:#1e40af,stroke:#1e40af,color:#ffffff
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 📘 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
**Define characteristic compressive strength of concrete. Explain how it differs from target mean strength.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** As per IS 456:2000 (Cl. 2.1), *characteristic compressive strength ($f_{ck}$)* of concrete is defined as the value of the cube compressive strength below which **not more than 5%** of the test results are expected to fall. In other words, it is the strength value having a **95% confidence level** that the actual strength of concrete exceeds this value.

> **Difference from Target Mean Strength (2 marks):** Since concrete is a heterogeneous material, individual test results vary statistically. To ensure that **at least 95% of samples meet $f_{ck}$**, the **target mean strength** ($f_{m}$) used at the mix design stage is set *higher* than $f_{ck}$ by a margin of $1.65 \sigma$ (where $\sigma$ is the standard deviation of test results, typically $3.5$ to $5.0$ N/mm² for good control):
>
> $$f_m = f_{ck} + 1.65 \, \sigma$$

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2023]**
**What is the split tensile strength of concrete? State its significance and the relevant IS code.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** Split tensile strength ($f_{ct}$) is the **indirect tensile strength** of concrete, determined by the **Brazilian test** (IS 5816:1999), in which a standard concrete cylinder (150 mm dia × 300 mm length) is loaded diametrically in compression along its entire length until it splits along the vertical plane.

> **Formula (1 mark):**
> $$f_{ct} = \dfrac{2P}{\pi D L}$$
> where $P$ = failure load, $D$ = diameter, $L$ = length.

> **Significance (1 mark):** It is a **convenient measure of tensile strength** of concrete (since direct tension tests are difficult to perform), used to estimate the **shear capacity** of concrete in beams (IS 456 uses $0.85\sqrt{f_{ck}}$ for design shear), **crack resistance** of pavements and slabs, and is empirically related to compressive strength by $f_{ct} \approx 0.35\sqrt{f_{ck}}$.

---

### 📗 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Style Question]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the procedure for determining the **compressive strength** of concrete as per IS 516. State the apparatus, specimen size, loading rate, and the formula used. Discuss **3 factors** affecting compressive strength.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Derive the relation between **Modulus of Elasticity ($E_c$) and Characteristic Compressive Strength ($f_{ck}$)** as per IS 456:2000. A concrete of grade M30 has a density of 25 kN/m³. Calculate its short-term and long-term modulus of elasticity (assume creep coefficient = 1.6).

---

### **Model Solution for Question A**

#### Part (a) — Compressive Strength Test (7 marks)

**Procedure as per IS 516 (3 marks):**

1. **Specimen Preparation:** Cast concrete cubes of size **150 mm × 150 mm × 150 mm** in standard moulds. Compact using table vibrator or rodding (35 strokes per layer, 3 layers).
2. **Curing:** After 24 hours, demould and immerse cubes in clean, fresh water at **27 ± 2 °C** for **28 days**.
3. **Testing Apparatus:** **Compression Testing Machine (CTM)** of capacity ≥ 2000 kN, with two parallel steel platens (hardness ≥ HV 600).
4. **Loading Rate:** Apply load **uniformly and continuously** at rate of **14 N/mm² per minute** (≈ 140 kg/cm²/min) until the specimen fails.
5. **Record Failure Load:** Note peak load $P_{\max}$ in kN.

**Formula (1 mark):**

$$
f_{ck} = \dfrac{P_{\max}}{A} = \dfrac{P_{\max}}{22500} \; \text{N/mm}^{2}
$$

where $A = 150 \times 150 = 22{,}500 \; \text{mm}^{2}$.

**Three Factors Affecting Compressive Strength (3 marks):**

> 1. **Water-Cement Ratio (Abram's Law):** Lower w/c → higher strength (provided workability is maintained).
> 2. **Age of Concrete:** Strength increases with age — at 7 days ≈ 65% of 28-day strength; at 90 days ≈ 115%.
> 3. **Type of Cement & Curing Conditions:** OPC gains strength faster than PPC; inadequate curing reduces strength by 20–40%.

#### Part (b) — Modulus of Elasticity Derivation & Calculation (7 marks)

**Derivation Logic (3 marks):**
IS 456 Cl. 6.2.3.1 states for **short-term static modulus of elasticity of normal-weight concrete**:

$$
E_c = 5000 \, \sqrt{f_{ck}} \; \text{N/mm}^{2}
$$

This empirical equation is derived from extensive experimental data regression analysis, where the relation was found to be of the form $E_c \propto \sqrt{f_{ck}}$ with proportionality constant **5000** for concrete of density **~24–25 kN/m³**.

For **short-term modulus**, $E_s = E_c$.

For **long-term (sustained) modulus**:

$$
E_s = \dfrac{E_c}{1 + \theta}
$$

where $\theta$ = creep coefficient.

**Numerical Problem (4 marks):**

Given:
- Grade = M30 → $f_{ck} = 30$ N/mm²
- Density = 25 kN/m³ (close to normal weight, so IS formula applies)
- Creep coefficient $\theta = 1.6$

**Step 1 — Calculate $E_c$:**

$$
E_c = 5000 \times \sqrt{30} = 5000 \times 5.4772 = 27{,}386 \; \text{N/mm}^{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Short-term modulus (creep not considered):**

$$
E_{s,\text{short}} = E_c = 27{,}386 \; \text{N/mm}^{2} \approx 27.39 \; \text{GPa}
$$

**Step 3 — Long-term modulus:**

$$
E_{s,\text{long}} = \dfrac{E_c}{1 + \theta} = \dfrac{27{,}386}{1 + 1.6} = \dfrac{27{,}386}{2.6}
$$

$$
E_{s,\text{long}} = 10{,}533 \; \text{N/mm}^{2} \approx 10.53 \; \text{GPa}
$$

**Final Answer:**

$$
\boxed{E_{s,\text{short}} \approx 27{,}386 \; \text{N/mm}^{2}, \quad E_{s,\text{long}} \approx 10{,}533 \; \text{N/mm}^{2}}
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style Question]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat sketch, describe the **split tensile strength test** of concrete. Derive the formula $f_{ct} = 2P/(\pi D L)$. What are the limitations of this test?

**(b) [7 Marks]** A concrete mix design with w/c = 0.50 yielded a 28-day compressive strength of 30 MPa. If the w/c ratio is reduced to 0.40, estimate the new strength using **Abram's Law**. State the practical considerations when reducing the w/c ratio at site.

---

### **Model Solution for Question B**

#### Part (a) — Split Tensile Strength Test (7 marks)

**Neat Sketch Description (2 marks):**
A standard cylinder ($D = 150$ mm, $L = 300$ mm) is placed horizontally between the platens of CTM. Two **thin plywood strips** (width = 12 mm, thickness = 3 mm, length ≥ L) are placed top and bottom along the diametrical plane. Load is applied at uniform rate of **1.2 to 2.4 N/mm²/min** until the cylinder splits into two halves along the loaded plane.

**Derivation of Formula (3 marks):**
According to 2D elasticity theory (Hertzian contact solution), a diametral line load $P$ applied along the length of a cylinder produces a **uniform horizontal tensile stress** across the loaded diameter:

$$
\sigma_t = \dfrac{2P}{\pi D L}
$$

Concrete, being **~10× weaker in tension**, fails by **horizontal splitting** along the vertical plane (not by compression). Hence:

$$
f_{ct} = \dfrac{2P}{\pi D L}
$$

**Limitations (2 marks):**

> 1. The biaxial stress state (compressive vertical + tensile horizontal) does **not truly represent pure tension** — the actual failure stress may differ from $f_{ct}$ theoretically.
> 2. Stress distribution assumes **isotropic, homogeneous, linearly elastic** material — concrete is neither.
> 3. **Result depends on specimen geometry** — different $D/L$ ratios give different results.
> 4. **Edge effects** near the loading strips may cause premature compressive failure.

#### Part (b) — Abram's Law Numerical (7 marks)

**Given Data:**
- Initial: $w/c = 0.50$, $f_1 = 30$ MPa
- Final: $w/c = 0.40$, $f_2 = ?$
- Age = 28 days

**Step 1 — Using Abram's Law (4 marks):**
$$
f_c = \dfrac{A}{B^{\,w/c}}
$$

For the initial condition:

$$
30 = \dfrac{A}{B^{0.50}}
$$

For the new condition:

$$
f_2 = \dfrac{A}{B^{0.40}}
$$

**Step 2 — Take the Ratio (Eliminates A):**

$$
\dfrac{f_2}{30} = \dfrac{A / B^{0.40}}{A / B^{0.50}} = B^{0.50 - 0.40} = B^{0.10}
$$

**Step 3 — Find B from Initial Condition (assuming standard $A = 984$ MPa):**

$$
B^{0.50} = \dfrac{A}{30} = \dfrac{984}{30} = 32.8
$$

$$
B = (32.8)^{2} = 1075.84
$$

$$
B^{0.10} = (1075.84)^{0.10} \approx 2.05
$$

**Step 4 — Calculate $f_2$:**

$$
f_2 = 30 \times 2.05 \approx 61.5 \; \text{MPa}
$$

**Practical Considerations when Reducing w/c at Site (3 marks):**

> 1. **Workability suffers** — at $w/c = 0.40$, the mix becomes harsh and stiff; **plasticizers (superplasticizers)** must be added to maintain slump.
> 2. **Compaction becomes critical** — low w/c mixes require **vibratory compaction**; hand compaction leads to honeycombing.
> 3. **Higher cement content** is needed to compensate for lower w/c, increasing **heat of hydration** — risk of thermal cracking in mass concrete.
> 4. **Risk of drying shrinkage cracks** if curing is not maintained.
> 5. **Cost increase** due to superplasticizers and additional cement.

**Final Answer:**

$$
\boxed{f_2 \approx 61.5 \; \text{MPa} \;\; (\text{about } 2 \text{ times the strength at } w/c = 0.50)}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark-Loss Points:**
> 1. **Confusing $E_c$ with $E_s$:** Students often forget to divide by $(1 + \theta)$ for long-term modulus — losing **2–3 marks**.
> 2. **Using 150 mm cylinder area as 22,500 mm²:** The cylinder area is $\pi D^2/4 = 17{,}671$ mm². **Don't mix cube and cylinder areas!**
> 3. **Split tensile formula misuse:** Writing $f_{ct} = P/A$ (where $A$ is cylinder area) instead of $f_{ct} = 2P/(\pi DL)$ — fatal error.
> 4. **Forgetting units:** Always express $E_c$ in N/mm² or GPa consistently.
> 5. **Abram's Law assumptions:** Remember it applies only to **plastic mixes** of **workable consistency** — not for very low w/c where compaction is poor (the strength vs w/c curve actually **flattens or dips** below $w/c = 0.35$ due to honeycombing!).

---

### 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Hardened concrete** properties are evaluated at **28 days** under standardized test conditions (IS 516).
- [x] **Compressive strength** is the **king property** — it governs **grade designation (M20, M25, M30... M60)**.
- [x] **Cube test (150 mm)** is standard in India; cylinder test gives ≈ **0.8 × cube strength**.
- [x] **Modulus of Elasticity (IS 456):** $E_c = 5000\sqrt{f_{ck}}$ — only for **normal-weight concrete (~24–25 kN/m³)**.
- [x] **Short-term modulus:** $E_s = E_c$; **Long-term (sustained) modulus:** $E_s = E_c / (1+\theta)$.
- [x] **Split tensile strength** uses Brazilian test (cylinder, IS 5816): $f_{ct} = 2P/(\pi DL)$.
- [x] **Flexural strength** uses **150×150×700 mm beam**; **center-point loading:** $f_{cr} = 3PL/(2bd^{2})$; **third-point loading:** $f_{cr} = PL/(bd^{2})$.
- [x] **Empirical relations:** $f_{ct} \approx 0.35\sqrt{f_{ck}}$; $f_{cr} \approx 0.7\sqrt{f_{ck}}$.
- [x] **Abram's Law:** $f_c = A / B^{(w/c)}$ — **lower w/c → higher strength**, but only up to a practical limit (~0.35).
- [x] **w/c ratio is the master variable** — controls strength, durability, workability, and shrinkage simultaneously.
- [x] **Standard loading rates:** Cube = 14 N/mm²/min; Flexure = 1.2–2.4 N/mm²/min.
- [x] **Standard curing temperature:** 27 ± 2 °C in water.
- [x] **Target mean strength:** $f_m = f_{ck} + 1.65\sigma$.
- [x] **Normal concrete density:** 23–25 kN/m³; **lightweight concrete** uses $E_c = 2000\sqrt{f_{ck}}$.

---
