# 📓 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Style]**

### **Q1. Differentiate between Nominal Mix and Design Mix of concrete.** `[3 Marks]`

**Model Answer:**

| Basis | **Nominal Mix** | **Design Mix** |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Fixed volumetric ratio of ingredients prescribed by IS 456 Table 9 (e.g., 1 : 2 : 4 for M15). | Scientifically calculated proportions based on target strength, workability, and durability (IS 10262 : 2019). |
| Basis of selection | Empirical (past experience, code table). | Performance-based (laboratory trial). |
| Grades | Permitted up to **M20** (RCC) per IS 456. | Recommended for **all grades**, mandatory for M25 and above. |
| Trial mix | Not required. | **Mandatory** — minimum three trial batches. |
| Economy | May use excess cement (over-conservative). | Cement content optimised → typically 5–15 % saving. |
| Use | Small residential, mass housing. | Bridges, high-rise, prestressed, marine, industrial. |

---

### **Q2. List the variables that affect concrete mix proportioning.** `[3 Marks]`

**Model Answer:**

The variables of mix proportioning are:

1. **Water–cement ratio** – primary strength-controlling variable.
2. **Cement content and type** – OPC 33/43/53, PPC, PSC, SRPC.
3. **Maximum size of aggregate (MSA)** – affects water demand.
4. **Grading of fine aggregate** – Zone I to IV (IS 383).
5. **Shape and texture of aggregate** – rounded/angular, smooth/rough.
6. **Workability requirement** – slump/compaction factor for the given placing method.
7. **Type and dosage of admixtures** – plasticizers, retarders, accelerators, mineral admixtures.
8. **Environmental exposure condition** – mild/moderate/severe/very severe/extreme.
9. **Target strength and durability** – characteristic strength and IS 456 durability limits.
10. **Method of placing** – manual/pumped/tremie.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> *Note: In KTU ESE, students must answer ONE full question from a choice of TWO. Each 14-mark question typically has two sub-parts of 7 marks each.*

---

### 🔹 **Question A — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]`

**(a)** Explain the **concept of concrete mix design** with its objectives. List the various **variables of proportioning** that influence the design. `[7 Marks]`

**(b)** Design a concrete mix of grade **M30** using **IS 10262 : 2019** guidelines for the following data:
- Maximum size of aggregate = **20 mm**
- Slump required = **75 mm**
- Specific gravity of cement = **3.15**; FA = **2.65**; CA = **2.74**
- Exposure condition = **Moderate**
- Fine aggregate belongs to **Zone II**
- Standard deviation = **5.0 MPa** (assumed, no prior data)
- Use **OPC 53** cement, no admixture.
- Assume entrapped air = **2 %**

Determine the mix proportions per m³ of concrete. `[7 Marks]`

---

#### **Model Solution (a) — Concept & Variables (7 Marks)**

**Concept of Mix Design:**
Concrete mix design is the **process of selecting suitable ingredients of concrete** (cement, water, fine aggregate, coarse aggregate, and admixtures) and determining their **relative proportions** to produce concrete of required **strength, durability, and workability** at minimum cost.

**Objectives of Mix Design:**
1. To achieve the **specified characteristic strength** ($f_{ck}$) at 28 days.
2. To achieve adequate **workability** for the chosen method of placement and compaction.
3. To ensure **durability** under the given exposure conditions (minimum cement, max w/c).
4. To achieve **economy** — minimum cement content consistent with the performance requirements.
5. To minimise **shrinkage, creep, and heat of hydration** especially in mass concrete.

**Variables of Proportioning (minimum 5 expected for full marks):**
1. Water–cement ratio
2. Cement content
3. Maximum size of aggregate
4. Grading and shape of aggregate
5. Type of cement
6. Admixtures (chemical and mineral)
7. Workability requirement
8. Exposure condition
9. Method of placing

---

#### **Model Solution (b) — M30 Mix Design (7 Marks)**

**Step 1: Target Mean Strength**

From IS 10262:2019 Table 1, for M30 (no prior data): $S = 5.0$ MPa

$$f'_t = f_{ck} + 1.65 \, S = 30 + 1.65 \times 5.0 = 38.25 \, \text{MPa}$$

**Step 2: Selection of w/c ratio**

From IS 10262:2019 Figure 1, for $f'_t = 38.25$ MPa, $w/c \approx 0.43$

Durability check (IS 456, moderate exposure, RCC): max w/c = **0.50**. Since $0.43 < 0.50$ → **strength governs**.

$$\boxed{w/c = 0.43}$$

**Step 3: Water content (IS 10262:2019 Table 3)**

For 20 mm MSA and 75 mm slump, $w = 197 \, \text{kg/m}^3$ (interpolated between 50 mm and 100 mm values 186 and 200).

**Step 4: Cement content**

$$m_c = \frac{197}{0.43} = 458 \, \text{kg/m}^3$$

Check: IS 456 minimum for moderate exposure = **300 kg/m³** → $458 > 300$ ✓

**Step 5: Coarse aggregate ratio (Table 4, 20 mm MSA, Zone II)**

$$r = 0.62$$

**Step 6: Absolute volumes**

$$V_c = \frac{458}{3.15 \times 1000} = 0.1454 \, \text{m}^3$$

$$V_w = \frac{197}{1000} = 0.1970 \, \text{m}^3$$

$$V_{\text{air}} = 0.02 \, \text{m}^3$$

$$V_{\text{TA}} = 1.0 - (0.1454 + 0.1970 + 0.02) = 0.6376 \, \text{m}^3$$

**Step 7: Mass of CA and FA**

$$m_{\text{CA}} = 0.6376 \times 0.62 \times 2.74 \times 1000 = 1083.0 \, \text{kg/m}^3$$

$$m_{\text{FA}} = 0.6376 \times 0.38 \times 2.65 \times 1000 = 642.0 \, \text{kg/m}^3$$

**Final Mix Proportions per m³:**

| Ingredient | Mass (kg/m³) | Ratio |
|---|---|---|
| Cement | 458 | **1.00** |
| Water | 197 | **0.43** |
| Fine Aggregate | 642 | **1.40** |
| Coarse Aggregate | 1083 | **2.36** |

**Mix Ratio by weight: 1 : 1.40 : 2.36 (Cement : FA : CA), w/c = 0.43**

---

### 🔹 **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023 Style]`

**(a)** What are the **general considerations** that govern the selection of mix proportions? Explain the role of **standard deviation** in mix design. `[7 Marks]`

**(b)** A nominal mix of **M20 grade** concrete is to be replaced by a design mix. The data is:
- MSA = 40 mm, Slump = 25–50 mm, Exposure = Mild
- $G_c = 3.15$, $G_{\text{FA}} = 2.60$, $G_{\text{CA}} = 2.70$
- FA Zone = III, $S = 4.0$ MPa
- Entrapped air = **1.0 %** (40 mm MSA)

Determine the mix proportions. `[7 Marks]`

---

#### **Model Solution (a) — General Considerations (7 Marks)**

**General Considerations in Mix Proportioning:**

1. **Strength Requirement:** The 28-day characteristic compressive strength ($f_{ck}$) is the primary design parameter; target mean strength $f'_t = f_{ck} + 1.65 S$ is adopted.

2. **Durability Requirement:** The mix must satisfy minimum cement content and maximum w/c ratio as per IS 456 Table 5 based on exposure condition. The **more restrictive of the two** (strength-based and durability-based) governs.

3. **Workability Requirement:** Adequate slump/compaction factor must be ensured for the method of placing; otherwise honeycombing and voids occur.

4. **Maximum Size of Aggregate (MSA):** Limited by member dimension, cover, and reinforcement spacing. Larger MSA reduces water demand and improves economy.

5. **Grading & Quality of Aggregate:** Conforming to IS 383; controls paste demand and workability.

6. **Type of Cement and Admixtures:** Affect strength gain rate, durability, and workability.

7. **Trial Mix Verification:** Mandatory laboratory trial with 3 cubes each at 3, 7, 28 days.

**Role of Standard Deviation ($S$):**
- $S$ measures the **inherent variability** of strength in a given set of production conditions.
- Higher $S$ → greater safety margin ($1.65 S$ added to $f_{ck}$).
- Lower $S$ (good quality control) → economical mix.
- $S$ is obtained from **at least 30 prior test results**; if not available, IS 10262:2019 Table 1 gives assumed values.

---

#### **Model Solution (b) — M20 Design Mix (7 Marks)**

**Step 1: Target Mean Strength**

$$f'_t = 20 + 1.65 \times 4.0 = 26.6 \, \text{MPa}$$

**Step 2: w/c ratio**

From IS 10262:2019 Figure 1 for 26.6 MPa: $w/c \approx 0.55$. Durability check (mild, RCC): max w/c = 0.60 → **strength governs**.

$$w/c = 0.55$$

**Step 3: Water content (Table 3, 40 mm MSA, 50 mm slump)**

$w = 165 \, \text{kg/m}^3$

**Step 4: Cement content**

$$m_c = \frac{165}{0.55} = 300 \, \text{kg/m}^3$$

Check: IS 456 minimum (mild, RCC) = 300 kg/m³ → **just adequate**.

**Step 5: CA ratio (Table 4, 40 mm MSA, Zone III)**

$r = 0.69$ (FA Zone III coarser → less fine, more coarse)

**Step 6: Absolute volumes**

$$V_c = \frac{300}{3.15 \times 1000} = 0.0952 \, \text{m}^3$$

$$V_w = \frac{165}{1000} = 0.1650 \, \text{m}^3$$

$$V_{\text{air}} = 0.01 \, \text{m}^3$$

$$V_{\text{TA}} = 1.0 - (0.0952 + 0.1650 + 0.01) = 0.7298 \, \text{m}^3$$

**Step 7: Mass of CA and FA**

$$m_{\text{CA}} = 0.7298 \times 0.69 \times 2.70 \times 1000 = 1359.6 \, \text{kg/m}^3$$

$$m_{\text{FA}} = 0.7298 \times 0.31 \times 2.60 \times 1000 = 588.1 \, \text{kg/m}^3$$

**Final Mix Proportions per m³:**

| Ingredient | Mass (kg/m³) | Ratio |
|---|---|---|
| Cement | 300 | **1.00** |
| Water | 165 | **0.55** |
| Fine Aggregate | 588 | **1.96** |
| Coarse Aggregate | 1360 | **4.53** |

**Mix Ratio by weight: 1 : 1.96 : 4.53 (Cement : FA : CA), w/c = 0.55**

> Compare with nominal 1 : 1.5 : 3 → design mix uses **4.53 parts CA** (much higher MSA = 40 mm justifies this), saving cement.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks in Mix Design questions (verified by KTU board patterns):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to add $1.65 S$** → most common. Target mean strength is **NOT** the characteristic strength.
> 2. **Using $V_{\text{air}} = 0$** → IS 10262 mandates 2 % (20 mm) and 1 % (40 mm) entrapped air. This single error shifts every mass by 1–2 %.
> 3. **Confusing volumetric ratio with mass ratio** → Nominal mixes (IS 456) are *volumetric*; Design mixes (IS 10262) are *by mass*.
> 4. **Using durability w/c vs strength w/c wrongly** → Always adopt the **lower (more restrictive)** w/c.
> 5. **Skipping the trial mix step** → 14-mark questions without the trial-mix mention lose 2–3 marks.
> 6. **Wrong CA ratio from Table 4** → Students pick the wrong row; it depends on **MSA AND FA Zone simultaneously**.
> 7. **Forgetting to convert kg to volume correctly** → $V = m / (G \times 1000)$, not $m/G$.
> 8. **Stating "ratio = 1 : 1.5 : 3" as a design mix** → that is a **nominal mix** for M20; design mix has unique numerical ratio.

---

## 5.4 🎯 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

- [x] **Mix Proportioning** = selecting quantities of cement, water, FA, CA, admixtures.
- [x] **Mix Design** = systematic engineering process (IS 10262:2019) → yields a **Design Mix**.
- [x] **Nominal Mix** = fixed volumetric ratio from IS 456 Table 9 → used for grades up to M20.
- [x] **Design Mix** = calculated mix by mass, mandatory trial, ≥ M25 → economises cement by 5–15 %.
- [x] **5 key variables**: w/c ratio, cement content, water content, MSA, FA grading (plus admixtures, exposure).
- [x] **$f'_t = f_{ck} + 1.65 S$** (target mean strength, IS 10262:2019).
- [x] **Abrams' Law**: $f_c = A / B^{w/c}$ — strength falls steeply as w/c rises.
- [x] **Bolomey equation**: $f_{28} = k_1 (1/(w/c) - k_2)$.
- [x] **9-step IS 10262:2019 procedure**: target strength → w/c → water → cement → CA ratio → absolute volume → masses → trial mix → finalisation.
- [x] **Absolute Volume Method**: $\sum V_i = 1 \, \text{m}^3$ where $V_i = m_i / (G_i \times 1000)$.
- [x] **Standard IS assumptions**: 2 % air for 20 mm MSA, 1 % for 40 mm MSA; SSD condition of aggregate.
- [x] **Trial mix is non-negotiable** for design mix — 3 cubes each at 3, 7, 28 days.
- [x] **Cement content durability check**: ≥ 300 kg/m³ (RCC mild), ≥ 300 kg/m³ (RCC moderate), 320 (severe), 340 (very severe), 360 (extreme).
- [x] **w/c durability check**: ≤ 0.60 (mild), 0.55 (moderate), 0.50 (severe), 0.45 (very severe), 0.40 (extreme).
- [x] **Remember**: design mix proportions are expressed as **mass per m³**, NOT as a single ratio string.

> 🏁 **End of Module 3 – Mix Proportioning Notes**

# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition of Concrete Mix Design

**Concrete Mix Design** is the science and engineering process of selecting suitable proportions of cement, water, fine aggregate (sand), coarse aggregate (gravel), and admixtures to produce concrete of desired **strength**, **durability**, and **workability** at minimum cost.

As per the KTU 2024 syllabus and IS 10262:2019, mix design is fundamentally a **proportioning problem** governed by two criteria:

1. **Strength Criterion (Structural):** The 28-day characteristic compressive strength ($f_{ck}$) must be achieved.
2. **Durability Criterion (Environmental):** The maximum water-cement ratio and minimum cement content specified in IS 456:2000 (Table 5) for the given exposure condition must be satisfied.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition to Memorize:** *"Concrete mix design is the process of determining the relative proportions of concrete ingredients to achieve a specified minimum strength, durability, and workability economically."*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bakery Recipe" Approach

Imagine you are a baker creating a cake:
- You have **flour** (cement — the binder),
- **Sugar & butter** (fine aggregate — fills small gaps),
- **Chocolate chips** (coarse aggregate — bulk skeleton),
- **Milk/eggs** (water — makes it workable), and
- **Baking powder** (admixtures — special performance).

If you add too much milk, the cake becomes weak and porous. If you add too little, you cannot pour the batter. **Mix design is exactly this balancing act**, except that "tasting" the cake is replaced by a **150 mm cube crushing test at 28 days**.

> [!NOTE]
> **The golden rule of concrete:** Water is the **necessary evil** — it activates cement hydration but leaves capillary pores that weaken the hardened matrix. Therefore, the *water-cement ratio* is the single most important parameter in mix design.

## 1.3 Objectives of Mix Design

A properly designed concrete mix must satisfy the following:

- **Strength** — meet the specified characteristic compressive strength at 28 days.
- **Workability** — be easily mixed, transported, placed, compacted, and finished without segregation.
- **Durability** — withstand the environmental exposure (chloride, sulphate, freeze-thaw, etc.).
- **Economy** — minimum cost per cubic metre of concrete.
- **Density** — suitable for plain, reinforced, or prestressed construction.

## 1.4 Various Methods of Mix Design (Worldwide)

Different countries have standardized their own methods. The most common methods are:

| Method | Country / Standard | Underlying Philosophy |
|---|---|---|
| **IS Method** | India — IS 10262:2019 | Statistical strength concept + absolute volume method |
| **ACI Method** | USA — ACI 211.1 | Empirical tables based on aggregate size and slump |
| **DOE Method** | UK — Department of Environment | Statistical + absolute volume method |
| **BS Method** | Britain — BS 5328 | Similar to DOE |
| **FCT (USBR)** | USA — Bureau of Reclamation | Trial-mix based on max density curve |
| **NSCP (Philippines)** | Philippines — NSCP | ACI adaptation |
| **European EN 206** | Europe | Performance-based + prescribed mix options |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For KTU examinations, **IS 10262:2019** is the *only* method you need to know in detail. The comparative mention of ACI/DOE is to give context only.

## 1.5 The IS 10262:2019 Philosophy — A Two-Stage Filter

The Indian Standard approach uses a **two-stage check**:

$$f_{ck,target} = f_{ck} + 1.65\,\sigma \quad \text{(Strength Filter)}$$

$$(w/c)_{selected} = \min\left[(w/c)_{strength},\,(w/c)_{durability}\right] \quad \text{(Durability Filter)}$$

The final w/c is the **lower (more conservative) of the two** ratios. This guarantees both strength and durability.

## 1.6 Standard Reference Constants Used in IS 10262:2019

- Specific gravity of Ordinary Portland Cement (OPC) ≈ **3.15**
- Specific gravity of water at 4 °C ≈ **1.00**
- Specific gravity of fine aggregate ≈ **2.60 to 2.70** (typical)
- Specific gravity of coarse aggregate ≈ **2.65 to 2.80** (typical)
- Standard deviation σ: assumed based on production control grade (IS 10262 Table 1)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Memory Anchor:** Mix design is a *trail* of decisions. Each step is a checkpoint. If you skip a step, you cannot reach the final mix.

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The 12-Step Framework of IS 10262:2019

The IS code prescribes a structured approach. The complete step-by-step logic is as follows:

### **STEP 1 — Data Collection (Inputs Required)**

Before you start, you must know:
- Characteristic compressive strength $f_{ck}$ (MPa) — from structural designer.
- Type of cement (OPC 43, OPC 53, PPC, PSC) — Table 1 of IS 456.
- Maximum nominal size of aggregate (MSA) — typically 10 mm, 20 mm, or 40 mm.
- Exposure condition — Mild, Moderate, Severe, Very Severe, Extreme (IS 456 Table 3).
- Workability requirement (slump in mm) — from structural element type.
- Specific gravities of cement, fine aggregate (FA), coarse aggregate (CA).
- Water absorption of aggregates.
- Type of aggregate (angular, sub-angular, rounded, flaky).
- Admixture type and dosage.
- Quality control expected — "Good", "Fair", or "Poor" (determines σ).

### **STEP 2 — Target Mean Strength ($f'_c$)**

The mix is proportioned for a *higher* target strength than the characteristic strength to statistically account for variability:

$$f'_c = f_{ck} + 1.65\,\sigma$$

where:
- $f'_c$ = Target mean compressive strength at 28 days (MPa)
- $f_{ck}$ = Characteristic compressive strength specified (MPa)
- $\sigma$ = Standard deviation (MPa) — from IS 10262:2019 Table 1

**Standard Deviation Table (IS 10262:2019 Table 1):**

| Grade of Concrete | Good Control (MPa) | Fair Control (MPa) |
|---|---|---|
| M10, M15 | 3.5 | 4.5 |
| M20, M25 | 4.0 | 5.0 |
| M30, M35 | 5.0 | 6.0 |
| M40, M45 | 6.0 | 7.0 |
| M50, M60 | 7.0 | 8.0 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> If the standard deviation is not known, the values in the "Fair Control" column shall be assumed, as per IS 10262:2019 Clause 5.3.1.

### **STEP 3 — Selection of Water-Cement Ratio (w/c)**

Two w/c ratios are determined and the **lower one** is adopted.

**(a) Strength-based w/c ratio:**
From IS 10262:2019 Table 2 (also called the Abrams' curve relation), based on $f'_c$ and type of cement.

**(b) Durability-based w/c ratio:**
From IS 456:2000 Table 5, based on exposure condition and type of structure (RCC or PCC).

**Selected w/c ratio:**

$$\left(\frac{w}{c}\right)_{final} = \min\left[\left(\frac{w}{c}\right)_{strength},\,\left(\frac{w}{c}\right)_{durability}\right]$$

### **STEP 4 — Selection of Water Content**

Water content depends on:
- Nominal maximum size of aggregate (MSA)
- Required workability (slump)
- Type of aggregate (angular / sub-angular / rounded)
- Use of admixtures / plasticizers

**Base water content for 75 mm slump, 20 mm MSA, angular aggregate (IS 10262:2019 Table 4) = 186 kg/m³**

**Corrections:**

- For every ±25 mm change in slump → adjust water by ±3 %
- For rounded aggregate → reduce water by **15 kg/m³**
- For 10 mm MSA → multiply by **1.20**
- For 40 mm MSA → multiply by **0.85**
- If superplasticizer is used → reduce water as per dosage (typically 20–30 %).

### **STEP 5 — Calculation of Cement Content**

$$C = \frac{W}{(w/c)}$$

Then **check** against IS 456 minimum cement content for durability:

$$C_{final} = \max\left[\frac{W}{(w/c)},\;C_{min,\,IS\,456}\right]$$

### **STEP 6 — Coarse Aggregate Fraction (by Volume)**

IS 10262:2019 Table 5 gives the **volume of coarse aggregate per unit volume of total aggregate ($V_{CA}$)** for different w/c ratios and MSA, assuming angular aggregate and Zone II sand.

| w/c ratio | 10 mm | 20 mm | 40 mm |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0.40 | 0.50 | 0.62 | 0.71 |
| 0.45 | 0.49 | 0.61 | 0.70 |
| 0.50 | 0.48 | 0.60 | 0.69 |
| 0.55 | 0.47 | 0.59 | 0.68 |
| 0.60 | 0.46 | 0.58 | 0.67 |

**Corrections to $V_{CA}$:**

- For rounded aggregate → reduce $V_{CA}$ by **0.10**
- For Zone III sand → reduce $V_{CA}$ by **0.10**
- For Zone I sand → increase $V_{CA}$ by **0.10**

### **STEP 7 — Fine Aggregate Content (by Absolute Volume Method)**

The volume of fine aggregate is found by **subtracting the absolute volumes** of cement, water, coarse aggregate, and entrapped air from 1 m³.

$$V_{FA} = 1 - \left(V_C + V_W + V_{CA} + V_{air}\right)$$

Absolute volume is:

$$V = \frac{\text{Mass}}{\text{Specific Gravity} \times 1000} \quad \text{(m³ per m³ of concrete)}$$

### **STEP 8 — Mix Proportioning**

The final mix is expressed as the ratio:

$$C : FA : CA \;=\; 1 : \frac{FA}{C} : \frac{CA}{C}$$

with water-cement ratio $(w/c)$.

### **STEP 9 — Adjustment for Moisture and Absorption**

- If aggregates contain **surface moisture**, water to be added is *reduced*.
- If aggregates are *oven-dry* and *absorbent*, water to be added is *increased*.

$$W_{adjusted} = W_{batch} - q \times (M_s - A_s)$$

where $q$ = quantity of aggregate, $M_s$ = moisture content (%), $A_s$ = water absorption (%).

### **STEP 10 — Trial Mix Verification**

Three trial mixes are cast at ±10 % of selected w/c, cubes are tested at 28 days, and the mix is finalized.

### **STEP 11 — Reporting Mix Proportions**

Final answer is reported in kg per m³ of concrete AND as a ratio form.

### **STEP 12 — Cost Optimization (Optional)**

Trial-and-error with locally available materials to minimize cost.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $f'_c = f_{ck} + 1.65\sigma$ | Target mean strength | MPa |
| 2 | $C = W / (w/c)$ | Cement content | kg/m³ |
| 3 | $V_i = M_i / (G_i \times 1000)$ | Absolute volume of ingredient $i$ | m³/m³ |
| 4 | $\sum V_i + V_{air} = 1$ | Total volume = 1 m³ | m³ |
| 5 | $FA = V_{FA} \times G_{FA} \times 1000$ | Mass of fine aggregate | kg/m³ |
| 6 | $CA = V_{CA} \times G_{CA} \times 1000$ | Mass of coarse aggregate | kg/m³ |
| 7 | $W_{add} = W - \sum q_i(M_i - A_i)$ | Adjusted batch water | kg |
| 8 | $V_{air} = \% \text{ air} / 100$ | Entrapped air volume | m³ |
| 9 | $C_{min}$ from IS 456:2000 Table 5 | Min cement for durability | kg/m³ |
| 10 | $(w/c)_{max}$ from IS 456:2000 Table 5 | Max w/c for durability | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the formulas above, $G_i$ denotes the **specific gravity** of the ingredient. Never use the `vert` symbol inside table cells — always use `mid` for "divides" notation.

## 2.3 Entrapped Air Content (IS 10262:2019 Table 6)

| Nominal MSA (mm) | Entrapped air (% of volume) |
|---|---|
| 10 | 1.5 |
| 20 | 1.0 |
| 40 | 0.5 |

## 2.4 Why These Steps Matter — Engineering Reality

- **Step 2 (Target Strength):** Compensates for the fact that the cube strength varies, and the 5th percentile (1.65σ below mean) is the *characteristic* value.
- **Step 3 (Dual w/c):** Ensures the concrete does not fail by *strength* OR by *deterioration* — whichever is more critical.
- **Step 4 (Water Content):** Governs slump. More water → more slump → weaker concrete.
- **Step 6 (CA Fraction):** Controls the *packing* of the aggregate skeleton. Higher CA fraction → stiffer mix.
- **Step 7 (FA by Difference):** Ensures the voids left by CA + cement paste are exactly filled.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Target Mean Strength Equation

The statistical foundation of mix design begins with the assumption that cube strengths follow a **normal (Gaussian) distribution** with mean $\mu$ and standard deviation $\sigma$.

For a normal distribution, the value below which **5 %** of the test results lie is:

$$f_{ck} = \mu - 1.645\,\sigma \quad \text{(lower 5th percentile)}$$

The factor 1.645 corresponds to a one-tailed normal probability. IS 10262:2019 rounds it to **1.65** for engineering convenience.

Rearranging to find the mean $\mu$ (which we call the target mean strength $f'_c$):

$$f'_c = \mu = f_{ck} + 1.65\,\sigma \quad \blacksquare$$

This means: design the mix so that the *average* of test results is 1.65σ **above** the characteristic value, ensuring that less than 5 % of cubes fall below $f_{ck}$.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Absolute Volume Method

The **absolute volume method** (also called the **volumetric mix design**) is built on the principle that concrete is a *concentrated suspension* of solid particles filling a unit volume of 1 m³. The sum of the absolute volumes of all ingredients plus entrapped air must equal the total volume:

$$V_{cement} + V_{water} + V_{fine\,agg} + V_{coarse\,agg} + V_{air} = 1 \;\text{m}^3$$

For each ingredient $i$:

$$V_i = \frac{M_i}{G_i \times \rho_w}$$

where $M_i$ = mass of ingredient (kg), $G_i$ = specific gravity of ingredient, $\rho_w$ = density of water = **1000 kg/m³**.

Therefore:

$$\frac{M_c}{G_c \times 1000} + \frac{M_w}{G_w \times 1000} + \frac{M_{FA}}{G_{FA} \times 1000} + \frac{M_{CA}}{G_{CA} \times 1000} + V_{air} = 1$$

Since $G_w = 1$, this simplifies to:

$$\frac{M_c}{G_c} + M_w + \frac{M_{FA}}{G_{FA}} + \frac{M_{CA}}{G_{CA}} + 1000\,V_{air} = 1000$$

This is the **master equation** of absolute volume mix design.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** In the exam, you will often be told $M_c$, $M_w$, $M_{CA}$ and asked to find $M_{FA}$. Substitute everything into the master equation and solve.

## 3.3 Worked-Out Numerical Problem (Module 3 Standard)

**Problem (KTU University Exam style — 14 marks):**

> Design a concrete mix of grade **M30** using IS 10262:2019 for the following data:
> - Type of cement: OPC 53 (specific gravity = 3.15)
> - Maximum nominal size of aggregate: 20 mm
> - Specific gravity of fine aggregate (sand): 2.65
> - Specific gravity of coarse aggregate: 2.75
> - Exposure condition: Severe (RCC)
> - Required slump: 100 mm
> - Type of aggregate: Angular
> - Sand belongs to grading Zone II
> - Quality control: Good
> - Superplasticizer: 1 % by weight of cement (water reduction 25 %)

**Step A — Target Mean Strength:**

$$f'_c = f_{ck} + 1.65\,\sigma = 30 + 1.65 \times 5.0 = 30 + 8.25 = 38.25 \;\text{MPa}$$

**Step B — Selection of w/c ratio (Strength Basis):**

For OPC 53 cement, 28-day strength of 38.25 MPa corresponds (from IS 10262:2019 Table 2) to:

$$\left(\frac{w}{c}\right)_{strength} \approx 0.45$$

**Step C — Selection of w/c ratio (Durability Basis):**

For Severe exposure, RCC (IS 456:2000 Table 5):

$$\left(\frac{w}{c}\right)_{durability} = 0.45$$

(Note: maximum permissible w/c for severe exposure = 0.45)

**Step D — Selected w/c ratio:**

$$\left(\frac{w}{c}\right)_{final} = \min(0.45,\,0.45) = 0.45$$

**Step E — Selection of Water Content:**

Base water content (20 mm MSA, 75 mm slump, angular) from IS 10262:2019 Table 4 = 186 kg/m³.

Slump correction: Required slump = 100 mm → increase by 25 mm → increase water by 3 %.

$$W_{initial} = 186 \times 1.03 = 191.58 \;\text{kg/m}^3$$

Superplasticizer reduces water by 25 %:

$$W_{final} = 191.58 \times 0.75 = 143.69 \;\text{kg/m}^3$$

**Step F — Cement Content:**

$$C = \frac{W_{final}}{(w/c)} = \frac{143.69}{0.45} = 319.31 \;\text{kg/m}^3$$

Check against IS 456 minimum for severe exposure, RCC: $C_{min} = 320 \;\text{kg/m}^3$.

Since $319.31 < 320$, **revise cement to** $C_{final} = 320 \;\text{kg/m}^3$.

Revised w/c: $w/c = 143.69 / 320 = 0.449$ (no major change — round to 0.45).

**Step G — Coarse Aggregate Fraction:**

For 20 mm MSA and w/c = 0.45, IS 10262:2019 Table 5 gives:

$$V_{CA} = 0.61 \quad \text{(volume of CA per unit volume of total aggregate)}$$

(No correction needed for angular aggregate and Zone II sand.)

**Step H — Absolute Volume of Ingredients:**

Entrapped air for 20 mm MSA (IS 10262:2019 Table 6) = 1.0 % = 0.01 m³.

$$V_C = \frac{320}{3.15 \times 1000} = 0.10159 \;\text{m}^3$$

$$V_W = \frac{143.69}{1.0 \times 1000} = 0.14369 \;\text{m}^3$$

$$V_{CA} \times 1 = \text{Volume of CA in 1 m}^3 = 0.61 \times (V_{FA} + V_{CA})$$

Let $V_{TA}$ = total volume of aggregate = $V_{FA} + V_{CA}$. Then $V_{CA} = 0.61\,V_{TA}$.

Using the absolute volume equation:

$$V_C + V_W + V_{CA} + V_{FA} + V_{air} = 1$$

$$0.10159 + 0.14369 + V_{CA} + V_{FA} + 0.01 = 1$$

$$V_{CA} + V_{FA} = 1 - 0.10159 - 0.14369 - 0.01 = 0.74472 \;\text{m}^3$$

So $V_{TA} = 0.74472 \;\text{m}^3$.

Therefore:

$$V_{CA} = 0.61 \times 0.74472 = 0.45428 \;\text{m}^3$$

$$V_{FA} = 0.74472 - 0.45428 = 0.29044 \;\text{m}^3$$

**Step I — Masses of Aggregates:**

$$CA = 0.45428 \times 2.75 \times 1000 = 1249.27 \;\text{kg/m}^3$$

$$FA = 0.29044 \times 2.65 \times 1000 = 769.66 \;\text{kg/m}^3$$

**Step J — Mix Ratio:**

$$C : FA : CA = 320 : 769.66 : 1249.27$$

Dividing by 320:

$$C : FA : CA = 1 : 2.40 : 3.90 \quad \text{with } w/c = 0.45$$

**Step K — Final Mix Per m³ of Concrete (Solution Summary):**

| Ingredient | Quantity (kg/m³) |
|---|---|
| Cement (OPC 53) | 320 |
| Water | 143.7 |
| Fine Aggregate (sand) | 769.7 |
| Coarse Aggregate (20 mm) | 1249.3 |
| Superplasticizer (1 %) | 3.2 |
| **Total** | **2485.9** |
| Entrapped air | 1 % (10 L) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Mix Proportion (by mass):** **1 : 2.40 : 3.90** with $w/c = 0.45$ and admixture @ 1 % of cement weight.

| Variable ↑ | w/c | Water | Cement | FA | CA | Strength | Workability | Cost |

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Flow Architecture of IS 10262:2019 Mix Design Procedure

The mix design procedure can be visualized as a multi-stage decision process with feedback loops.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[INPUT DATA: fck, MSA, exposure, slump, Gc, Gfa, Gca, type] --> B[Step 1: Target Mean Strength f'c = fck + 1.65 sigma]
    B --> C[Step 2a: w/c from strength Table 2]
    A --> D[Step 2b: w/c from durability IS 456 Table 5]
    C --> E[Step 2c: w/c final = MIN of two values]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Step 3: Base water content from Table 4]
    F --> G[Step 3a: Apply corrections for slump, MSA, aggregate type, admixture]
    G --> H[Step 4: Cement C = W divided by w/c]
    H --> I[Step 4a: Check Cmin from IS 456 Table 5]
    I --> J{Is C greater than Cmin?}
    J -- Yes --> K[Use C as final]
    J -- No --> K2[Adopt Cmin and recompute w/c]
    K2 --> K
    K --> L[Step 5: Vca from Table 5 with corrections for sand zone and aggregate shape]
    L --> M[Step 6: Absolute volume balance: Vc + Vw + Vfa + Vca + Vair equals 1]
    M --> N[Step 7: Solve for Vfa]
    N --> O[Step 8: Convert volumes to masses: M equals V times G times 1000]
    O --> P[Step 9: Express mix as ratio 1:FA:CA with w/c]
    P --> Q[Step 10: Apply moisture and absorption corrections to batch water]
    Q --> R[Step 11: Trial mix casting at plus or minus 10 percent w/c]
    R --> S[Step 12: 28 day cube test verification]
    S --> T[Final Mix Approved]
```

## 4.3 Decision Tree for w/c Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Choose w/c ratio] --> B[Compute w/c from strength curve Table 2]
    A --> C[Look up w/c from IS 456 Table 5 by exposure]
    B --> D{w/c strength less than w/c durability?}
    C --> D
    D -- Yes --> E[Adopt w/c from strength table]
    D -- No --> F[Adopt w/c from durability table]
    E --> G[Final w/c selected]
    F --> G
    G --> H{Check minimum cement content Cmin}
    H --> I[If C less than Cmin, increase C to Cmin]
    H --> J[If C greater than Cmin, use computed C]
    I --> K[Final mix with corrected w/c]
    J --> K
```

## 4.4 Comparative Schematic — IS 10262 vs ACI 211

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph IS[IS 10262:2019 METHOD]
        IS1[Statistical target strength] --> IS2[Dual w/c check strength and durability]
        IS2 --> IS3[Absolute volume method]
        IS3 --> IS4[Mix expressed in kg per m3 plus ratio]
    end
    subgraph ACI[ACI 211.1 METHOD]
        ACI1[Required slump and MSA] --> ACI2[Estimate water and air from table]
        ACI2 --> ACI3[Select w/c from strength vs durability table]
        ACI3 --> ACI4[Coarse aggregate from dry rodded unit weight]
        ACI4 --> ACI5[Fine aggregate by absolute volume difference]
    end
    IS --- SIM[Similarities: both use w/c and absolute volume]
    IS --- DIF1[Difference: IS uses statistical sigma, ACI uses slump and air tables]
    ACI --- SIM
    ACI --- DIF1
```

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks Equivalent)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, CO3, BTL: Remember]`

**State the two main criteria governing the proportioning of a concrete mix as per IS 10262:2019.**

**Model Answer:**

The two main criteria for proportioning a concrete mix are:

1. **Strength Criterion (Structural):** The 28-day characteristic compressive strength $f_{ck}$ of the mix must satisfy the target mean strength, calculated as $f'_c = f_{ck} + 1.65\,\sigma$. This ensures that at least 95 % of the cube test results exceed $f_{ck}$.

2. **Durability Criterion (Environmental):** The maximum water-cement ratio and minimum cement content, as prescribed in IS 456:2000 Table 5, must be satisfied for the given exposure condition (Mild, Moderate, Severe, Very Severe, or Extreme). This ensures the hardened concrete resists chemical attack, chloride ingress, freeze-thaw cycles, and abrasion.

The selected w/c ratio is the **lower of the two** values obtained from the strength and durability criteria, ensuring that both requirements are simultaneously met.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write "strength and workability" instead of "strength and durability." Workability is an additional desirable property but is *not* a primary proportioning criterion in IS 10262. Marks will be deducted for this error.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, CO3, BTL: Understand]`

**Why is the design mix proportioned for a strength higher than the specified characteristic strength?**

**Model Answer:**

Concrete is a heterogeneous material whose compressive strength varies from batch to batch and from cube to cube due to inherent variability in:
- Quality of raw materials
- Mixing, transporting, placing, and curing practices
- Testing procedures

Statistical theory states that compressive strength follows a **normal (Gaussian) distribution**. The characteristic strength $f_{ck}$ is defined as the strength value below which **not more than 5 %** of the test results are expected to fall.

Therefore, the average (mean) of all test results — called the **target mean strength $f'_c$** — must be set *higher* than $f_{ck}$ by an amount equal to **1.65 standard deviations**:

$$f'_c = f_{ck} + 1.65\,\sigma$$

If the mix is designed only for $f_{ck}$, then roughly 50 % of cubes will fall *below* $f_{ck}$, violating the design intent. Designing for $f'_c$ ensures the contractual strength criterion is statistically guaranteed.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Do not confuse "target mean strength" with "characteristic strength." They are different by 1.65σ. Many students write $f'_c = f_{ck}$ in error.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, CO3, BTL: Apply/Analyse]`

> Design a concrete mix of grade **M25** using IS 10262:2019. Given:
> - Cement: OPC 43, specific gravity 3.10
> - Maximum nominal size of aggregate: 20 mm (angular)
> - Fine aggregate: river sand, Zone II, specific gravity 2.60
> - Coarse aggregate: crushed granite, specific gravity 2.70
> - Exposure: Moderate (RCC)
> - Slump required: 50 mm
> - Quality control: Fair
> - No admixture used
> - Entrapped air: 1 %
>
> **Sub-part (a) [7 marks]:** Calculate target mean strength and select the final w/c ratio.
> **Sub-part (b) [7 marks]:** Determine the cement, water, fine aggregate, and coarse aggregate content per m³ of concrete.

---

#### **Sub-part (a) Model Solution [7 marks]:**

**Step 1: Target Mean Strength [3 marks]**

For M25 grade with "Fair" control, IS 10262:2019 Table 1 gives:

$$\sigma = 5.0 \;\text{MPa}$$

$$f'_c = f_{ck} + 1.65\,\sigma = 25 + 1.65 \times 5.0 = 25 + 8.25 = 33.25 \;\text{MPa}$$

**Step 2: w/c from Strength [2 marks]**

From IS 10262:2019 Table 2 (curve between $f'_c$ and w/c for OPC 43), for $f'_c = 33.25$ MPa:

$$\left(\frac{w}{c}\right)_{strength} \approx 0.50$$

**Step 3: w/c from Durability [1.5 marks]**

For Moderate exposure, RCC (IS 456:2000 Table 5):

$$\left(\frac{w}{c}\right)_{durability} = 0.50$$

**Step 4: Final w/c [0.5 mark]**

$$\left(\frac{w}{c}\right)_{final} = \min(0.50,\,0.50) = 0.50$$

---

#### **Sub-part (b) Model Solution [7 marks]:**

**Step 5: Water Content [1.5 marks]**

Base water for 20 mm MSA, 75 mm slump, angular aggregate (IS 10262:2019 Table 4) = 186 kg/m³.

Required slump = 50 mm (< 75 mm), so reduce by 3 %:

$$W = 186 \times 0.97 = 180.42 \;\text{kg/m}^3 \approx 180 \;\text{kg/m}^3$$

**Step 6: Cement Content [1.5 marks]**

$$C = \frac{W}{(w/c)} = \frac{180}{0.50} = 360 \;\text{kg/m}^3$$

Check IS 456 minimum for moderate exposure, RCC: $C_{min} = 300$ kg/m³. Since $360 > 300$, **OK**.

**Step 7: Coarse Aggregate Fraction [1.5 marks]**

For 20 mm MSA and w/c = 0.50 (IS 10262:2019 Table 5):

$$V_{CA} = 0.60$$

No correction needed (angular aggregate, Zone II sand).

**Step 8: Absolute Volume Method [2.5 marks]**

Absolute volumes:

$$V_C = \frac{360}{3.10 \times 1000} = 0.11613 \;\text{m}^3$$

$$V_W = \frac{180}{1.0 \times 1000} = 0.18000 \;\text{m}^3$$

$$V_{air} = 0.01 \;\text{m}^3$$

Volume equation:

$$0.11613 + 0.18000 + 0.01 + V_{CA} + V_{FA} = 1$$

$$V_{CA} + V_{FA} = 0.69387 \;\text{m}^3$$

Since $V_{CA} = 0.60 \times (V_{CA} + V_{FA}) = 0.60 \times 0.69387 = 0.41632$ m³:

$$V_{FA} = 0.69387 - 0.41632 = 0.27755 \;\text{m}^3$$

**Step 9: Masses [1 mark]**

$$CA = 0.41632 \times 2.70 \times 1000 = 1124.06 \;\text{kg/m}^3$$

$$FA = 0.27755 \times 2.60 \times 1000 = 721.63 \;\text{kg/m}^3$$

**Step 10: Mix Ratio [0.5 mark]**

$$C : FA : CA = 360 : 721.63 : 1124.06 = 1 : 2.00 : 3.12 \;\text{with } w/c = 0.50$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students use the *volume of CA* in m³ directly without multiplying by specific gravity and 1000 to get mass. Always convert: $\text{Mass} = V \times G \times 1000$ kg/m³.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, CO3, BTL: Apply/Analyse]`

> Discuss the various methods of concrete mix design. Explain in detail the **IS 10262:2019 method** with a flowchart of the procedure. Highlight the differences between the **IS method** and the **ACI method**.
>
> **Sub-part (a) [7 marks]:** Explain the step-by-step procedure of IS 10262:2019 with a flow chart.
> **Sub-part (b) [7 marks]:** Compare the IS method with the ACI method and list the merits and demerits of each.

---

#### **Sub-part (a) Model Solution [7 marks]:**

**1. Various Methods of Mix Design [2 marks]**

- **IS 10262 Method (India):** Statistical strength concept + absolute volume method.
- **ACI 211.1 Method (USA):** Empirical tables based on MSA, slump, and air content.
- **DOE Method (UK):** Statistical + absolute volume method, similar to IS.
- **FCT/USBR Method:** Trial mixes based on max density curves.
- **BS Method:** Similar to DOE method.

**2. Step-by-Step Procedure of IS 10262:2019 [5 marks]**

1. Collect data: $f_{ck}$, exposure, MSA, slump, specific gravities, type of aggregate.
2. Compute target mean strength: $f'_c = f_{ck} + 1.65\,\sigma$.
3. Select w/c ratio from strength curve (Table 2) and from durability (IS 456 Table 5); adopt the **lower** value.
4. Select water content from Table 4; apply corrections for slump, MSA, shape, and admixtures.
5. Compute cement content: $C = W/(w/c)$; verify against $C_{min}$ from IS 456 Table 5.
6. Find coarse aggregate fraction $V_{CA}$ from Table 5; correct for sand zone and aggregate shape.
7. Apply absolute volume equation to solve for fine aggregate volume:

$$V_{FA} = 1 - \left(V_C + V_W + V_{CA} + V_{air}\right)$$

8. Convert volumes to masses using specific gravities.
9. Express as mix ratio and report per m³.
10. Adjust for moisture/absorption in aggregates.
11. Conduct trial mixes at ±10 % w/c and verify 28-day strength.

**3. Flow Chart [Embedded in SECTION 4 — refer to flowchart]**

---

#### **Sub-part (b) Model Solution [7 marks]:**

| Aspect | IS 10262:2019 | ACI 211.1 |
|---|---|---|
| Origin | India (BIS) | USA (American Concrete Institute) |
| Basis | Statistical strength concept | Empirical tables |
| Target strength | $f'_c = f_{ck} + 1.65\,\sigma$ | Strength based on w/c table |
| w/c selection | Strength curve + durability limit (IS 456 Table 5) | Strength and exposure table |
| Coarse aggregate | Volume fraction from Table 5 | Dry-rodded unit weight method |
| Fine aggregate | Absolute volume method | Absolute volume method |
| Water content | Based on MSA, slump, shape (kg/m³) | Based on MSA, slump, air (lb/yd³) |
| Admixtures | Considered as water reducer | Considered as chemical admixtures |
| Air content | Entrapped air only | Entrapped and entrained (for freeze-thaw) |
| Output units | kg/m³ | kg/m³ (or lb/yd³) |

**Merits of IS 10262:2019 [2 marks]:**
- Statistically rigorous
- Considers durability via IS 456
- Uses absolute volume — accounts for specific gravities
- Suited to Indian materials

**Demerits of IS 10262:2019 [1 mark]:**
- Requires standard deviation from past records
- Trial mixes mandatory
- Many corrections make it lengthy

**Merits of ACI 211.1 [1 mark]:**
- Simple, easy to use
- Quick initial estimate
- Wide empirical database

**Demerits of ACI 211.1 [1 mark]:**
- Less statistically rigorous
- Dry-rodded unit weight not always available

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** When asked to "compare," students often just list features of one method. The examiner expects a *side-by-side comparative table* AND a separate section on merits/demerits. Marks are awarded for structured comparison.

---

## 5.3 KTU 2024 Scheme — Topic Recap & Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Summary for Last-Minute Revision**

### **Key Concepts**
- ✅ Mix design = proportioning of ingredients to achieve **strength + durability + workability + economy**.
- ✅ IS 10262:2019 uses **statistical concept** (1.65σ) for target mean strength.
- ✅ **Two-stage w/c filter:** strength-based w/c AND durability-based w/c; take the lower.
- ✅ **Water content** depends on MSA, slump, aggregate shape, and admixtures.
- ✅ **Cement content** = water ÷ w/c, then check against IS 456 $C_{min}$.
- ✅ **Coarse aggregate volume** from IS 10262 Table 5 with corrections.
- ✅ **Fine aggregate** found by **absolute volume method** (the master equation).
- ✅ **Final mix** expressed in kg/m³ AND as ratio 1 : FA/C : CA/C with w/c.
- ✅ **Trial mix** verification at 28 days is essential before bulk use.

### **Must-Remember Constants**
- ✅ Standard deviation σ (Table 1, IS 10262:2019): for M25/M20 with Good control = **4.0 MPa**; Fair control = **5.0 MPa**.
- ✅ Entrapped air: 10 mm = 1.5 %, 20 mm = 1.0 %, 40 mm = 0.5 %.
- ✅ Base water for 20 mm MSA, 75 mm slump, angular = **186 kg/m³**.
- ✅ Correction for rounded aggregate: **−15 kg/m³ water** and **−0.10 to $V_{CA}$**.
- ✅ Specific gravity of OPC = **3.15** (often rounded to 3.10–3.15).

### **The Master Equation (Always Appears in Exam)**

$$V_C + V_W + V_{FA} + V_{CA} + V_{air} = 1 \;\text{m}^3$$

where $V_i = M_i / (G_i \times 1000)$.

### **Common Student Mistakes to Avoid**
- ❌ Using $f_{ck}$ directly without adding 1.65σ.
- ❌ Forgetting to check $C_{min}$ from IS 456.
- ❌ Using the higher w/c of the two values (it should be the **lower**).
- ❌ Not converting volume to mass using specific gravity.
- ❌ Forgetting to include entrapped air in the absolute volume equation.
- ❌ Confusing IS 10262 (mix design) with IS 456 (code of practice).
- ❌ Writing "kg of cement per bag" (50 kg) instead of "kg per m³".
- ❌ Not reporting the final mix in **both** ratio form and kg/m³.

### **Quick-Recall Formula Table (Print and Stick on Wall)**

| # | Formula | When to Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $f'_c = f_{ck} + 1.65\,\sigma$ | Step 1: Target mean strength |
| 2 | $(w/c)_{final} = \min(w/c_S,\,w/c_D)$ | Step 2: Final w/c |
| 3 | $C = W / (w/c)$ | Step 4: Cement content |
| 4 | $V_i = M_i / (G_i \times 1000)$ | Step 7: Volume conversion |
| 5 | $V_C + V_W + V_{FA} + V_{CA} + V_{air} = 1$ | Master equation |
| 6 | $M_i = V_i \times G_i \times 1000$ | Convert volume to mass |

### **Last-Minute Mnemonic — "TWD-CAS"**
- **T** = Target strength
- **W** = Water content selection
- **D** = Durability check
- **C** = Cement content
- **A** = Aggregate allocation (CA from table, FA by difference)
- **S** = Solve and report (ratio + kg/m³)

### **Exposure Conditions & Limits (IS 456:2000 Table 5 — Excerpt)**

| Exposure | Max w/c (RCC) | Min Cement (RCC) |
|---|---|---|
| Mild | 0.55 | 300 kg/m³ |
| Moderate | 0.50 | 300 kg/m³ |
| Severe | 0.45 | 320 kg/m³ |
| Very Severe | 0.45 | 340 kg/m³ |
| Extreme | 0.40 | 360 kg/m³ |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Final Note:** For 14-mark design problems, always present your answer in a **tabular summary form** (kg per m³ for each ingredient). Examiners reward clarity and structure. Show the *flow* of calculations, not just the final answer. A well-labelled solution with boxed key results will *always* score higher than a scrambled working sheet.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Statistical Quality Control of Concrete - Mean Strength, Standard Deviation, Coefficient of Variation, Sampling and Testing, Acceptance Criteria
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET412 - CONCRETE TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Statistical Quality Control of Concrete - Mean Strength, Standard Deviation, Coefficient of Variation, Sampling and Testing, Acceptance Criteria

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Statistical Quality Control (SQC) of Concrete — Formal Definition

Statistical Quality Control of concrete is a systematic engineering methodology that applies **statistical principles and probabilistic techniques** to evaluate, monitor, and control the variability of concrete strength. It transforms concrete from a material judged by single-test results into a **quality-assured, predictable engineering product** characterized by measurable parameters.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> Statistical Quality Control of Concrete is the application of statistical methods (Mean, Standard Deviation, Coefficient of Variation) to the compressive strength data of concrete cubes/cylinders, used to define target mean strength, evaluate producer's quality, and establish acceptance/rejection criteria as per **IS 456:2000** (Plain and Reinforced Concrete — Code of Practice) and **IS 1343:1980** (Prestressed Concrete).

### 1.2 The Four Pillars of Statistical Quality Control

The SQC framework rests on **four statistical parameters** that together define concrete quality:

| # | Parameter | Engineering Role |
|---|-----------|------------------|
| 1 | **Mean Strength ($f_m$)** | Average long-term strength capability of the concrete mix |
| 2 | **Standard Deviation ($\sigma$)** | Magnitude of inherent scatter in strength results |
| 3 | **Coefficient of Variation (CV)** | Relative measure of dispersion (normalized measure of quality) |
| 4 | **Characteristic Strength ($f_{ck}$)** | Design strength below which not more than 5% of test results are expected to fall |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Statistics?**
> Concrete is a heterogeneous material. No two batches, even with identical mix proportions, will yield exactly the same strength. The variability arises from fluctuations in cement quality, water content, aggregate grading, mixing time, compaction, curing, and testing. Statistics quantifies this variability and allows the engineer to design **safely**.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Coffee Shop Quality Analogy

Imagine a popular café that promises every cup of coffee will contain exactly **50 grams of espresso shot**. The barista weighs each shot, but due to slight variations in grind, tamping pressure, and pouring speed, the actual weight fluctuates between **47 g and 53 g**.

- **Mean Strength (f̄)** → The *average* espresso weight measured over a week.
- **Standard Deviation (σ)** → The *spread* around that average. A high σ means the barista is inconsistent.
- **Coefficient of Variation (CV)** → The *relative* inconsistency. A σ of 2 g on 50 g is less alarming than a σ of 2 g on 10 g.
- **Characteristic Strength (f_ck)** → The *guaranteed minimum* the café promises to customers. The barista must target higher than this so that even the weakest 5% of cups meet the promise.

Just as a good café monitors shot weights, a concrete engineer monitors cube strengths using statistics to ensure every batch of concrete placed on site meets design strength.

### 1.4 The Probabilistic Basis — Why 5%?

Statistical Quality Control rests on the **Normal (Gaussian) Distribution** of concrete strength. The 5% defective threshold comes from a probabilistic consideration:

$$P(X < f_{ck}) = \int_{-\infty}^{f_{ck}} \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} \, e^{-\frac{(x - f_m)^2}{2\sigma^2}} \, dx = 0.05$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 1.65 Multiplier**
> Solving the standard normal equation $P(Z < -1.645) = 0.05$ gives the well-known multiplier **1.65**. This is the bridge between the designer's *characteristic strength* and the producer's *target mean strength*. Many textbooks and codes round this to **1.64** or **1.65** — both are accepted in KTU examinations.

### 1.5 Standard Metrics Summary (in Bold)

- **Mean Strength ($f_m$)** — arithmetic average of test results, expressed in **MPa** or **N/mm²**.
- **Standard Deviation ($\sigma$)** — root mean square deviation from mean, expressed in **MPa**.
- **Coefficient of Variation (CV)** — ratio of $\sigma$ to $f_m$, expressed as a **percentage (%)**.
- **Characteristic Strength ($f_{ck}$)** — strength below which not more than **5%** of test results fall, expressed in **MPa**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Mean Strength ($f_m$) — The Centerline of Quality

**Definition:** The arithmetic mean of all compressive strength test results of a concrete mix, calculated from at least 30 consecutive test results (or as specified by IS 456).

**Formula:**

$$f_m = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} f_i$$

where:
- $f_m$ = mean compressive strength (MPa)
- $f_i$ = individual test result (MPa)
- $n$ = number of test results

**Engineering Significance:**
- Represents the *central tendency* of the strength population.
- A higher $f_m$ indicates the mix is producing strong concrete on average.
- However, a high mean alone is **insufficient** — a mix can have a high mean but a low σ if it is inconsistent.

**How to Calculate (Logic Steps):**
1. Cast and test a minimum of 30 cubes (or as per project requirements).
2. Record the 28-day compressive strength of each cube.
3. Sum all individual strength values.
4. Divide the total by the number of samples.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Highlight:** When fewer than 30 results are available, IS 456:2000 permits the use of results from at least **10 to 29 consecutive test results** for provisional target mean strength computation, but at least 30 results are required for a reliable σ estimate.

---

### 2.2 Standard Deviation ($\sigma$) — The Spread of Quality

**Definition:** Standard Deviation is a measure of the dispersion or scatter of individual strength results about the mean strength. A low σ indicates consistent, uniform quality; a high σ indicates erratic, unpredictable quality.

**Formula (Sample Standard Deviation — used in practice):**

$$\sigma = \sqrt{\frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(f_i - f_m)^2}{n-1}}$$

where:
- $\sigma$ = standard deviation (MPa)
- $f_i$ = individual test result (MPa)
- $f_m$ = mean strength (MPa)
- $n$ = number of test results
- $n-1$ = Bessel's correction (degrees of freedom)

**Alternative computational formula (preferred for hand calculation):**

$$\sigma = \sqrt{\frac{\sum f_i^2 - n \cdot f_m^2}{n-1}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why $n-1$ and not $n$?**
> We are working with a *sample* (not the entire population of all concrete ever made). Dividing by $n-1$ (Bessel's correction) gives an *unbiased estimator* of population standard deviation. For large $n$ (say $n > 30$), the difference is negligible.

**Engineering Significance of Standard Deviation:**
- **Low σ (< 3.5 MPa)** → Excellent quality control (typical of ready-mix plants with automated batching).
- **Medium σ (3.5 – 4.5 MPa)** → Good control (typical of well-managed site batching).
- **High σ (4.5 – 5.5 MPa)** → Fair control (typical of average site batching).
- **Very high σ (> 5.5 MPa)** → Poor control — mix proportioning or testing must be improved.

**IS 456:2000 Assumed Standard Deviation (Clause 9.2.4.1):**

| Grade of Concrete | Assumed Standard Deviation (MPa) |
|:-----------------:|:--------------------------------:|
| M10, M15          | 3.5                              |
| M20, M25          | 4.0                              |
| M30 and above     | 5.0                              |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** These values are used *only* when adequate test data is not available. Once 30+ test results are available, the *actual* calculated σ must be used.

---

### 2.3 Target Mean Strength ($f_{target}$ or $f'_m$)

**Definition:** The strength that the mix designer must target so that the characteristic strength is achieved with the desired probability (95%).

**Formula (IS 456:2000, Clause 9.2.4.2):**

$$f_{target} = f_{ck} + 1.65 \cdot \sigma$$

This equation is the **cornerstone of mix design under SQC**. It raises the target above the design strength by an amount proportional to the variability of production.

**Worked Numerical Insight:**
- If $f_{ck} = 25$ MPa and $\sigma = 4$ MPa → $f_{target} = 25 + 1.65 \times 4 = 25 + 6.6 = 31.6$ MPa.
- The mix must be designed for 31.6 MPa, not 25 MPa, to ensure that 95% of cubes will achieve ≥ 25 MPa.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often write $f_{target} = f_{ck} + 1.65\sigma$ but forget to convert units or use the wrong σ value. Always verify the σ used is the *actual* σ from test data (or the *assumed* σ as per IS 456 table when test data is unavailable).

---

### 2.4 Coefficient of Variation (CV) — The Normalized Quality Index

**Definition:** Coefficient of Variation is the ratio of standard deviation to mean strength, expressed as a percentage. It allows comparison of quality across different concrete grades.

**Formula:**

$$CV = \frac{\sigma}{f_m} \times 100 \; (\%) $$

**Engineering Significance:**
- CV is **dimensionless** (in percentage) and is the most reliable indicator of *quality level*.
- It is independent of the absolute strength, so a M20 mix and an M60 mix can be compared on the same scale.

**CV Classification as per IS 456:2000 (Clause 9.2.4.1 — interpreted from general practice):**

| Quality Level | Coefficient of Variation (%) |
|:-------------:|:----------------------------:|
| Excellent     | Below 10%                    |
| Very Good     | 10 – 15%                     |
| Good          | 15 – 20%                     |
| Fair          | 20 – 25%                     |
| Poor          | Above 25%                    |

---

### 2.5 Sampling and Testing (IS 456:2000, Clause 15)

#### 2.5.1 Sampling Frequency
The code prescribes the **minimum frequency of sampling** for compressive strength tests:

| Quantity of Concrete (m³) | Number of Samples |
|:-------------------------:|:-----------------:|
| 1 – 5                     | 1                 |
| 6 – 15                    | 2                 |
| 16 – 30                   | 3                 |
| 31 – 50                   | 4                 |
| 51 and above              | 4 + 1 per additional 50 m³ (or part thereof) |

#### 2.5.2 Test Specimens per Sample
- Each sample consists of **6 cubes** (150 mm × 150 mm × 150 mm) for ordinary concrete.
- 3 cubes are tested at **7 days** (informative) and 3 cubes at **28 days** (acceptance criterion).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The 28-day compressive strength is the *acceptance* test. The 7-day test is only a *quality monitoring* indicator (typically $\approx$ 65–70% of 28-day strength for OPC).

#### 2.5.3 Standard Test Procedure
1. Cast cubes in clean, oiled moulds in **3 layers**, each compacted with **35 strokes** of a tamping rod (for 150 mm cube).
2. Cure in water at **27 ± 2 °C** for the specified age.
3. Test in a **calibrated compression testing machine** at the specified loading rate of **140 kg/cm²/min** ($\approx$ **13.7 N/mm²/min** or **5.2 kN/s** for 150 mm cube).

---

### 2.6 Acceptance Criteria (IS 456:2000, Clause 16.1)

The acceptance of concrete is based on the **compressive strength** of cubes tested at 28 days. The criteria are:

#### 2.6.1 Mandatory Acceptance Criterion

> **The mean strength determined from any group of four consecutive non-overlapping test results shall exceed the characteristic strength by at least 3 MPa (or 0.825 times σ, whichever is greater).**

Mathematically:

$$f_{4,mean} \geq f_{ck} + 3 \; \text{MPa} \quad \text{or} \quad f_{4,mean} \geq f_{ck} + 0.825\sigma$$

#### 2.6.2 Individual Result Criterion

> **No individual test result shall fall below $f_{ck}$ by more than 3 MPa.**

Mathematically:

$$f_i \geq f_{ck} - 3 \; \text{MPa}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Both criteria must be satisfied. A single failed criterion is grounds for rejection. Many students remember only the "3 MPa" rule but forget to apply both conditions.

---

### 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Comprehensive)

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Code Reference |
|---|-----------|:-------:|:-----:|:--------------:|
| 1 | Mean Strength | $f_m = \frac{\Sigma f_i}{n}$ | MPa | Statistical |
| 2 | Standard Deviation (direct) | $\sigma = \sqrt{\frac{\Sigma(f_i - f_m)^2}{n-1}}$ | MPa | IS 456:2000 |
| 3 | Standard Deviation (computational) | $\sigma = \sqrt{\frac{\Sigma f_i^2 - n f_m^2}{n-1}}$ | MPa | Statistical |
| 4 | Target Mean Strength | $f_{target} = f_{ck} + 1.65\sigma$ | MPa | IS 456 Cl. 9.2.4.2 |
| 5 | Coefficient of Variation | $CV = \frac{\sigma}{f_m} \times 100$ | % | Statistical |
| 6 | Variance | $\sigma^2 = \frac{\Sigma(f_i - f_m)^2}{n-1}$ | (MPa)² | Statistical |
| 7 | Standard Error | $SE = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$ | MPa | Statistical |
| 8 | Acceptance (Group of 4) | $f_{4m} \geq f_{ck} + 3$ (or $f_{ck} + 0.825\sigma$) | MPa | IS 456 Cl. 16.1 |
| 9 | Acceptance (Individual) | $f_i \geq f_{ck} - 3$ | MPa | IS 456 Cl. 16.1 |
| 10 | 1.65 σ Confidence Multiplier | 95% confidence, 5% defectives | — | Probabilistic |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mastery Tip:** Memorize items 1, 2, 4, 5, 8, and 9 — these alone account for 80% of KTU SQC problems.

### 2.8 Real-World Utility of SQC

Statistical Quality Control is the **backbone** of:
1. **Ready-Mix Concrete (RMC) plants** — daily QA/QC reports to clients.
2. **Large infrastructure projects** — bridges, dams, high-rise buildings.
3. **Quality audits** — third-party testing agencies (like SQAE, BMTPC).
4. **Forensic investigation** of failed structures — to assess if original concrete met specification.
5. **ISO 9001 certification** of concrete producers.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the "1.65 σ" Multiplier (Why 1.65?)

This is a **high-weightage KTU derivation question** (frequently asked for 5–7 marks).

**Step 1: Statement of the Probabilistic Requirement**

By definition of characteristic strength $f_{ck}$, we require that the probability of any individual concrete cube's strength being *less than* $f_{ck}$ is at most 5%:

$$P(X < f_{ck}) \leq 0.05$$

**Step 2: Assume Normal Distribution**

The compressive strength $X$ of concrete is assumed to follow a Normal (Gaussian) Distribution with mean $f_m$ and standard deviation $\sigma$:

$$X \sim N(f_m, \sigma^2)$$

**Step 3: Standardize the Variable**

Define the standard normal variable $Z$:

$$Z = \frac{X - f_m}{\sigma}$$

When $X = f_{ck}$:

$$Z = \frac{f_{ck} - f_m}{\sigma}$$

**Step 4: Express the Probability**

$$P(X < f_{ck}) = P\!\left(Z < \frac{f_{ck} - f_m}{\sigma}\right) = 0.05$$

**Step 5: Find $z_{0.05}$ from Standard Normal Tables**

From standard normal tables:

$$P(Z < -1.645) = 0.05$$

Therefore:

$$\frac{f_{ck} - f_m}{\sigma} = -1.645$$

**Step 6: Solve for $f_m$**

$$f_{ck} - f_m = -1.645 \cdot \sigma$$

$$f_m = f_{ck} + 1.645 \cdot \sigma$$

**Step 7: Round to Engineering Convention**

Rounding $1.645$ to $1.65$ (as used in IS 456:2000):

$$\boxed{f_{target} = f_m = f_{ck} + 1.65\sigma}$$

This completes the derivation. The factor **1.65** is a *universal constant* derived from the Normal Distribution at 5% area in one tail.

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Problem — Calculation of $\sigma$, $f_m$, CV, and $f_{target}$

> **Problem Statement (KTU Style):**
> The 28-day compressive strength test results (in MPa) of 10 concrete cubes cast from a single grade M25 mix are: **27.0, 29.5, 31.0, 26.5, 30.0, 32.5, 28.0, 30.5, 29.0, 33.0**.
> Calculate:
> (a) Mean strength $f_m$
> (b) Standard deviation $\sigma$
> (c) Coefficient of variation CV
> (d) Target mean strength $f_{target}$
> (e) Whether the mix satisfies IS 456 acceptance criteria for M25 grade.

**Solution:**

#### Part (a): Mean Strength

$$f_m = \frac{27.0 + 29.5 + 31.0 + 26.5 + 30.0 + 32.5 + 28.0 + 30.5 + 29.0 + 33.0}{10}$$

$$f_m = \frac{297.0}{10} = 29.70 \; \text{MPa}$$

#### Part (b): Standard Deviation (Direct Method)

Construct the deviation table:

| S.No. | $f_i$ (MPa) | $f_i - f_m$ | $(f_i - f_m)^2$ |
|:-----:|:-----------:|:-----------:|:---------------:|
| 1     | 27.0        | $-2.70$     | 7.29            |
| 2     | 29.5        | $-0.20$     | 0.04            |
| 3     | 31.0        | $+1.30$     | 1.69            |
| 4     | 26.5        | $-3.20$     | 10.24           |
| 5     | 30.0        | $+0.30$     | 0.09            |
| 6     | 32.5        | $+2.80$     | 7.84            |
| 7     | 28.0        | $-1.70$     | 2.89            |
| 8     | 30.5        | $+0.80$     | 0.64            |
| 9     | 29.0        | $-0.70$     | 0.49            |
| 10    | 33.0        | $+3.30$     | 10.89           |
| **Sum** | **297.0** | **0.00**  | **42.10**       |

Now compute:

$$\sigma = \sqrt{\frac{\sum(f_i - f_m)^2}{n-1}} = \sqrt{\frac{42.10}{10-1}} = \sqrt{\frac{42.10}{9}}$$

$$\sigma = \sqrt{4.6778} = 2.163 \; \text{MPa}$$

#### Part (c): Coefficient of Variation

$$CV = \frac{\sigma}{f_m} \times 100 = \frac{2.163}{29.70} \times 100$$

$$CV = 7.28 \;\%$$

> **Quality Assessment:** CV < 10% → **Excellent Quality Control** ✓

#### Part (d): Target Mean Strength

$$f_{target} = f_{ck} + 1.65 \sigma = 25 + 1.65 \times 2.163$$

$$f_{target} = 25 + 3.569 = 28.57 \; \text{MPa}$$

Since the actual mean $f_m = 29.70$ MPa $> 28.57$ MPa, the mix is *comfortably* achieving its target. ✓

#### Part (e): Acceptance Criteria Check

**Criterion 1 — Group of 4 Consecutive Results:** Take the first 4 results: 27.0, 29.5, 31.0, 26.5.

$$f_{4m} = \frac{27.0 + 29.5 + 31.0 + 26.5}{4} = \frac{114.0}{4} = 28.50 \; \text{MPa}$$

Required: $f_{4m} \geq f_{ck} + 3 = 25 + 3 = 28.0$ MPa. Since $28.50 \geq 28.0$ ✓

**Criterion 2 — Individual Result:** Minimum result = 26.5 MPa. Required: $f_i \geq f_{ck} - 3 = 25 - 3 = 22.0$ MPa. Since $26.5 \geq 22.0$ ✓

**Conclusion:** Both criteria are satisfied. The concrete is **ACCEPTED**. ✓

---

### 3.3 Derivation of Acceptance Criterion (Group of 4 + 3 MPa)

This is the **statistical logic** behind the "3 MPa" rule in IS 456.

**Step 1: Consider a group of 4 consecutive test results.**

The mean of 4 results is:

$$\bar{X}_4 = \frac{1}{4} \sum_{i=1}^{4} X_i$$

**Step 2: Distribution of the Mean**

The mean of 4 samples has standard error:

$$SE_{\bar{X}_4} = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{4}} = \frac{\sigma}{2} = 0.5\sigma$$

**Step 3: Probabilistic Requirement**

We require that the group mean exceed $f_{ck}$ with high probability. The factor **0.825 σ** represents:

$$z_{0.05} \times SE = 1.645 \times 0.5\sigma = 0.8225\sigma \approx 0.825\sigma$$

**Step 4: Build the Criterion**

$$f_{4m} \geq f_{ck} + 0.825\sigma$$

**Step 5: Compare with the 3 MPa Rule**

The code specifies that this must be **at least 3 MPa** above $f_{ck}$, because for low-strength concrete where $0.825\sigma < 3$ MPa, the absolute 3 MPa cushion is a safety net. Hence the final criterion:

$$\boxed{f_{4m} \geq f_{ck} + 3 \; \text{MPa} \quad \text{or} \quad f_{ck} + 0.825\sigma \quad \text{whichever is greater}}$$

---

### 3.4 Conversion Logic Summary Table

| Engineering Decision | Statistical Basis | IS 456 Code Reference |
|:--------------------:|:-----------------:|:---------------------:|
| Target mean strength | 5% defectives, 95% confidence | Cl. 9.2.4.2 |
| Assumed σ (no data) | Historical Indian data | Cl. 9.2.4.1, Table 11 |
| Group of 4 acceptance | Mean of 4 lies above $f_{ck} + 0.825\sigma$ | Cl. 16.1 |
| Individual result | No cube $< f_{ck} - 3$ MPa | Cl. 16.1 |
| Sampling frequency | 1 sample per 5–50 m³ | Cl. 15.2 |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Statistical Quality Control Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Concrete Production] --> B[Batch Mix Design]
    B --> C[Cast Cubes from Batch]
    C --> D[28-Day Compression Test]
    D --> E[Record Individual Strength fi]
    E --> F[Compile n >= 30 Test Results]
    F --> G[Calculate Mean Strength fm]
    G --> H[Calculate Standard Deviation sigma]
    H --> I[Calculate Coefficient of Variation CV]
    I --> J[Compute Target Mean Strength ft = fck + 1.65 sigma]
    J --> K{Acceptance Check}
    K --> L[Group of 4: f4m >= fck + 3 MPa]
    K --> M[Individual: fi >= fck - 3 MPa]
    L --> N{Both Criteria Met?}
    M --> N
    N -->|Yes| O[ACCEPT Concrete]
    N -->|No| P[REJECT or Investigate]
    P --> Q[Review Mix Proportions]
    Q --> B
    O --> R[Quality Report Issued]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — SQC Parameter Relationships

```mermaid
block-beta
    columns 3
    block:f1["Concrete Production Data (n cubes)"]
    block:f2["Individual Strength Results (fi)"]
    block:f3["Characteristic Strength (fck)"]
    f1 --> f2
    f3 --> f4["Target Mean Strength"]
    f2 --> f5["Mean Strength fm"]
    f2 --> f6["Standard Deviation sigma"]
    f5 --> f7["Coefficient of Variation CV"]
    f6 --> f4
    f6 --> f7
    f4 --> f8["Mix Design Verification"]
    f7 --> f9["Quality Rating: Excellent / Good / Fair / Poor"]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Acceptance Criteria Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Compressive Strength Test Results Available] --> Q[Group Results into Sets of 4 Consecutive]
    Q --> R[Compute Mean of Each Set of 4: f4m]
    R --> S{f4m >= fck + 3 MPa?}
    S -->|No| T[FAIL - Reject Batch]
    S -->|Yes| U{Any Individual fi < fck - 3 MPa?}
    U -->|Yes| T
    U -->|No| V[PASS - Accept Concrete]
    T --> W[Engineering Review and Re-testing]
    W --> X[Adjust Mix Design if Required]
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Sampling Frequency Logic (IS 456)

```mermaid
flowchart LR

    B -->|No| D{Q <= 15?}

    D -->|No| F{Q <= 30?}
    F -->|Yes| G[3 Samples]
    F -->|No| H{Q <= 50?}
    H -->|Yes| I[4 Samples]
    H -->|No| J[4 + 1 per Extra 50 m3]
    C --> K[6 Cubes per Sample]
    E --> K
    G --> K
    I --> K
    J --> K
    K --> L[3 Cubes at 7 Days, 3 at 28 Days]
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Left Tail: 2.5% Defective Area] --> B[Distribution Curve: Mean fm at Center]
    B --> C[Right Tail: 2.5% Defective Area]
    B --> D[Central 95% Region]
    D --> E[Lower 5% Boundary: fck Point]
    E --> F[Distance from fck to fm = 1.65 sigma]
    F --> G[Target Mean Strength Located]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Question 1 (3 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**

> Define the following terms with respect to Statistical Quality Control of concrete:
> (i) Mean strength
> (ii) Standard deviation
> (iii) Coefficient of variation

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Mean Strength ($f_m$):** The arithmetic average of the compressive strengths of a minimum of 30 (or as many available) concrete cube specimens tested at 28 days, expressed in MPa. It represents the central tendency of concrete strength.

$$f_m = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} f_i}{n}$$

**(ii) Standard Deviation ($\sigma$):** A statistical measure of the scatter or dispersion of individual strength results about the mean. A low σ indicates uniform and consistent concrete quality, while a high σ indicates erratic production.

$$\sigma = \sqrt{\frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(f_i - f_m)^2}{n-1}}$$

**(iii) Coefficient of Variation (CV):** The ratio of standard deviation to mean strength, expressed as a percentage. It is a *normalized* measure of variability and is used to compare the relative quality of concrete across different grades.

$$CV = \frac{\sigma}{f_m} \times 100 \; (\%)$$

A CV below 10% is considered *excellent* quality.

---

**Question 2 (3 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**

> State the acceptance criteria for the compressive strength of concrete as per IS 456:2000.

**Model Answer:**

As per **IS 456:2000 (Clause 16.1)**, the acceptance of concrete is based on 28-day compressive strength test results, and the concrete is deemed to comply with the specified characteristic strength $f_{ck}$ if **both** the following conditions are satisfied:

**Condition 1 — Group of 4 Consecutive Test Results:**
The mean strength of any group of **four non-overlapping consecutive test results** shall be at least:

$$f_{4m} \geq f_{ck} + 3 \; \text{MPa} \quad \text{or} \quad f_{4m} \geq f_{ck} + 0.825\sigma \;\; \text{(whichever is greater)}$$

**Condition 2 — Individual Test Result:**
No individual test result shall fall below $f_{ck}$ by more than 3 MPa:

$$f_i \geq f_{ck} - 3 \; \text{MPa}$$

Both conditions must be met. If either is violated, the concrete is **liable to rejection** and the structure must be investigated.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice Pattern)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for **target mean strength** of concrete as $f_{target} = f_{ck} + 1.65\sigma$. State clearly the underlying statistical assumption. (7 Marks)

**(b)** The 28-day compressive strength test results (in MPa) of 8 concrete cubes from a M30 grade mix are: **35.0, 38.5, 32.0, 36.0, 40.0, 33.5, 37.5, 39.5**. Calculate the mean strength, standard deviation, coefficient of variation, and target mean strength. Assess the quality level of the concrete based on CV. (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution to Question A:

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 Marks):**

**Step 1: Assumption:** The 28-day compressive strength of concrete follows a **Normal (Gaussian) Distribution** with mean $f_m$ and standard deviation $\sigma$.

$$X \sim N(f_m, \sigma^2)$$

**Step 2: Definition of Characteristic Strength:** The characteristic strength $f_{ck}$ is defined as the value of strength below which not more than 5% of test results are expected to fall:

$$P(X < f_{ck}) = 0.05$$

**Step 3: Standardization:** Define the standard normal variate:

$$Z = \frac{X - f_m}{\sigma}$$

When $X = f_{ck}$:

$$Z = \frac{f_{ck} - f_m}{\sigma}$$

**Step 4: Probability Expression:**

$$P\!\left(Z < \frac{f_{ck} - f_m}{\sigma}\right) = 0.05$$

**Step 5: From Standard Normal Tables:**

$$P(Z < -1.645) = 0.05$$

Therefore:

$$\frac{f_{ck} - f_m}{\sigma} = -1.645$$

**Step 6: Solving for $f_m$:**

$$f_m = f_{ck} + 1.645\sigma \approx f_{ck} + 1.65\sigma$$

$$\boxed{f_{target} = f_{ck} + 1.65\sigma}$$

**Hence Proved.**

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Step 1: Sum of Strengths**

$$\sum f_i = 35.0 + 38.5 + 32.0 + 36.0 + 40.0 + 33.5 + 37.5 + 39.5 = 292.0 \; \text{MPa}$$

**Step 2: Mean Strength**

$$f_m = \frac{292.0}{8} = 36.50 \; \text{MPa}$$

**Step 3: Sum of Squared Deviations**

| S.No. | $f_i$ | $f_i - f_m$ | $(f_i - f_m)^2$ |
|:-----:|:-----:|:-----------:|:---------------:|
| 1     | 35.0  | $-1.50$     | 2.25            |
| 2     | 38.5  | $+2.00$     | 4.00            |
| 3     | 32.0  | $-4.50$     | 20.25           |
| 4     | 36.0  | $-0.50$     | 0.25            |
| 5     | 40.0  | $+3.50$     | 12.25           |
| 6     | 33.5  | $-3.00$     | 9.00            |
| 7     | 37.5  | $+1.00$     | 1.00            |
| 8     | 39.5  | $+3.00$     | 9.00            |
| **Σ** |       |             | **58.00**       |

**Step 4: Standard Deviation**

$$\sigma = \sqrt{\frac{58.00}{8-1}} = \sqrt{\frac{58.00}{7}} = \sqrt{8.2857} = 2.879 \; \text{MPa}$$

**Step 5: Coefficient of Variation**

$$CV = \frac{2.879}{36.50} \times 100 = 7.89 \;\%$$

**Step 6: Target Mean Strength**

$$f_{target} = 30 + 1.65 \times 2.879 = 30 + 4.751 = 34.75 \; \text{MPa}$$

**Step 7: Quality Assessment**

Since $f_m = 36.50$ MPa $> f_{target} = 34.75$ MPa, the mix is achieving its target. The CV of **7.89%** is below 10%, indicating **EXCELLENT quality control** as per IS 456 standards.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a)** Explain the procedure for **sampling and testing of concrete** as per IS 456:2000. Include the frequency of sampling, number of specimens, curing conditions, and loading rate. (7 Marks)

**(b)** For a M25 grade concrete, the mean strength and standard deviation obtained from 30 test results are 32.0 MPa and 4.5 MPa respectively. Check whether the mix complies with IS 456:2000 acceptance criteria. Also, comment on the quality level based on CV. (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution to Question B:

**Part (a) — Sampling and Testing Procedure (7 Marks):**

**1. Frequency of Sampling (IS 456:2000, Clause 15.2):**
Samples shall be taken at the rate of **one sample per 5 m³** of concrete for small pours, scaling up to:
- 1 sample for 1–5 m³
- 2 samples for 6–15 m³
- 3 samples for 16–30 m³
- 4 samples for 31–50 m³
- 4 + 1 per additional 50 m³ (or part) for > 50 m³

**2. Test Specimens:**
Each sample consists of **6 cubes** of size 150 mm × 150 mm × 150 mm (or cylinders 150 mm dia × 300 mm height).

**3. Casting Procedure:**
Cubes are cast in clean, oiled moulds in **3 layers**, each compacted with **35 strokes** of a 16 mm dia tamping rod. Surface is finished smooth.

**4. Curing:**
After 24 hours, specimens are demoulded and cured in **clean fresh water at 27 ± 2 °C** until the test age (7 or 28 days).

**5. Testing Machine:**
A **calibrated compression testing machine** of appropriate capacity (typically 2000 kN) is used. The bearing surfaces must be clean.

**6. Loading Rate:**
Load is applied **uniformly and continuously** at a rate of:
- **140 kg/cm² per minute** (≈ 13.7 N/mm²/min or 5.2 kN/sec for 150 mm cube) until failure.

**7. Test Age:**
- **3 cubes** tested at 7 days (for information/monitoring).
- **3 cubes** tested at **28 days** (acceptance criterion).

**8. Strength Calculation:**
Compressive strength = Maximum load / Cross-sectional area, reported in MPa (N/mm²) to the nearest 0.5 MPa.

**Part (b) — Acceptance Check (7 Marks):**

**Given:** M25 concrete, $f_{ck} = 25$ MPa, $f_m = 32.0$ MPa, $\sigma = 4.5$ MPa, $n = 30$.

**Step 1: Target Mean Strength**

$$f_{target} = 25 + 1.65 \times 4.5 = 25 + 7.425 = 32.425 \; \text{MPa}$$

Since $f_m = 32.0$ MPa $< 32.425$ MPa, the mix is **slightly below target** — investigation warranted.

**Step 2: Coefficient of Variation**

$$CV = \frac{4.5}{32.0} \times 100 = 14.06 \;\%$$

This falls in the **10–15%** range, indicating **VERY GOOD** quality control.

**Step 3: Acceptance Criterion 1 (Group of 4)**

Required: $f_{4m} \geq f_{ck} + 3 = 25 + 3 = 28$ MPa (assuming $0.825\sigma = 3.71 > 3$, so the binding criterion is 3.71 MPa → use $f_{ck} + 0.825\sigma = 28.71$ MPa).

From the average of 30 results: $f_{4m,avg} = f_m = 32.0$ MPa $\geq 28.71$ MPa ✓

**Step 4: Acceptance Criterion 2 (Individual)**

Required: $f_i \geq 25 - 3 = 22$ MPa. Assuming all individual results lie in $f_m \pm 3\sigma = 32.0 \pm 13.5$, i.e., 18.5 to 45.5 MPa. Some results may be below 22 MPa — investigation needed.

**Conclusion:** Mean strength is *just below* the target (by 0.425 MPa). The mix needs minor refinement (e.g., reducing w/c ratio or improving aggregate grading) to consistently achieve $f_{target}$. Quality level: **Very Good** (CV ≈ 14%).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting Bessel's correction** — Students often compute $\sigma$ using $n$ instead of $n-1$ in the denominator, losing 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Confusing CV and σ** — CV is a percentage; σ is in MPa. Both must be calculated and interpreted separately.
> 3. **Skipping the 1.65 derivation steps** — KTU awards full marks only when the *standardization step* and *standard normal table lookup* are shown explicitly.
> 4. **Not applying BOTH acceptance criteria** — Always check both the group-of-4 condition AND the individual result condition.
> 5. **Forgetting to state the units (MPa) and code clause references** — These are *minor marks* but cumulatively significant.
> 6. **Mixing up sampling frequencies for cubes vs. cylinders** — IS 456 specifies cubes; cylinders are used only in special cases (e.g., lightweight concrete).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quick-Fire Revision — Memorize These Points**

- [x] **Mean Strength** = Sum of individual strengths ÷ Number of samples
- [x] **Standard Deviation** uses denominator **$(n-1)$** (Bessel's correction)
- [x] **Target Mean Strength** formula: $f_{target} = f_{ck} + 1.65\sigma$ — universally critical
- [x] **1.65** comes from 5% defectives (left tail) of Normal Distribution
- [x] **Coefficient of Variation** is always expressed in **percentage (not fraction)**
- [x] **CV < 10%** = Excellent; **10–15%** = Very Good; **15–20%** = Good; **> 25%** = Poor
- [x] Assumed σ for **M10–M15** = 3.5 MPa; **M20–M25** = 4.0 MPa; **M30+** = 5.0 MPa
- [x] Sampling: 1 sample per 5 m³ minimum, scaling up to 1 per 50 m³
- [x] **6 cubes per sample** — 3 at 7 days, 3 at 28 days
- [x] Curing temperature: **27 ± 2 °C** in fresh water
- [x] Loading rate: **140 kg/cm²/min** (or 13.7 N/mm²/min)
- [x] Cube size standard: **150 mm × 150 mm × 150 mm**
- [x] Acceptance requires **BOTH** conditions met:
  - Group of 4 mean $\geq f_{ck} + 3$ MPa (or $f_{ck} + 0.825\sigma$, whichever greater)
  - No individual result $< f_{ck} - 3$ MPa
- [x] **IS 456:2000** is the governing code — remember to cite clause numbers
- [x] **At least 30 test results** are required for reliable σ; 10–29 results are provisional

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Strategy:** For 14-mark questions, allocate marks as follows — 2 marks for definitions/formulas, 4 marks for derivation/derivation logic, 4 marks for numerical calculation, 2 marks for quality assessment, and 2 marks for conclusion/code reference. This breakdown mirrors KTU's expected answer structure.
